title: N+1 per-chunk content_hash SELECT in ingest_url dedup loop
description: For every chunk of an ingested page, ingest_url issues a separate `SELECT id FROM chunks WHERE content_hash = ?` round-trip inside the for-loop. A long article can produce dozens-to-hundreds of chunks, so this is a classic N+1 query pattern: N synchronous SQLite calls where one batched query would suffice. content_hash is UNIQUE/indexed (db.py:189) so each query is cheap, but the per-call Python/SQLite overhead and round-trips dominate for large pages. The module already exposes `_batched_execute` (used elsewhere for IN-clause batching under the 999-var limit) — the existing-hash set could be fetched in one (or a few batched) `WHERE content_hash IN (...)` queries, then membership checked in-memory.
suggested_fix: Collect all chunk hashes first, fetch existing rows in batched `SELECT id, content_hash FROM chunks WHERE content_hash IN ({ph})` calls via `_batched_execute`, build a {hash: id} dict, then iterate chunks checking the dict in memory instead of one query per chunk.

title: Repeated per-item content_hash SELECT in figure-insert loops
description: The same N+1 query pattern as ingest_url recurs in the three figure-insertion loops (`_extract_html_images`, `_extract_web_figures`, `_extract_element_captures`): each iteration runs `SELECT id FROM chunks WHERE content_hash = ?` to skip duplicates. These loops are bounded by `_MAX_IMAGES_PER_PAGE`/`_MAX_ELEMENT_CAPTURES` (10), so the absolute cost is small, but the pattern is identical and could be collapsed into a single batched existence query computed before the insert loop. Lower priority than the ingest_url loop purely because of the smaller bound.
suggested_fix: Hoist a single batched `SELECT content_hash FROM chunks WHERE content_hash IN (...)` before each insert loop and check membership in a set, mirroring the recommended ingest_url fix.

title: Off-screen element filter only catches top-left overflow, not right/bottom
description: The comment claims the check skips 'elements outside the viewport (negative coords or zero-area)', but `if box['x'] + w <= 0 or box['y'] + h <= 0` only rejects elements whose right edge is at/left of x=0 OR whose bottom edge is at/above y=0 — i.e. only elements pushed off the top-left. Elements positioned far to the right (x > 1280, the screenshot clip width) or far below (y > 8000, the clip height used at line 107) pass the filter and get individually screenshotted via el.screenshot(), even though they were never part of the captured page screenshot region. This is not a crash, but it (a) contradicts the stated intent, (b) can waste vision calls on off-clip elements, and (c) the 'zero-area' justification is inaccurate (zero-area is already excluded by the _MIN_ELEMENT_DIMENSION check at line 39). Note el.screenshot() scrolls the element into view, so it still works — the issue is filter intent vs. behavior, not a render failure.
suggested_fix: Either correct the comment to 'skip elements off the top-left of the page', or, if the intent is to bound captures to the screenshot clip, compare against the clip bounds: skip when box['x'] >= clip_width or box['y'] >= clip_height (and the existing top-left checks). Pull the clip dimensions (1280x8000) into shared constants so render and filter agree.

title: Zero-norm (None) embeddings silently inserted as vector-less figure chunks
description: `_embed_with_config` is documented to return None entries for texts whose embeddings had zero norm (ingest.py:88,100-102). The figure-insert loops zip `collected`/`valid_figures` with `embeddings` and pass `embedding=emb_vec` straight into `_insert_chunk` without a None guard. `_insert_chunk` tolerates None (it just skips `insert_chunk_vec`, ingest.py:179-180), so there is no crash — but the chunk is then stored with no vector and is only ever reachable via FTS5/BM25, never via vector/RRF search. This is a silent partial-failure: a figure description that failed to embed becomes a second-class, semantically-invisible chunk with no log line indicating the degradation. The text-ingest path in ingest.py proper has the same property, so this is a shared pattern rather than web-specific, but it is worth flagging because vision/figure descriptions are exactly the content most expected to be retrieved semantically.
suggested_fix: Where a missing vector matters, skip or log figures whose emb_vec is None (e.g. `if emb_vec is None: logger.warning('zero-norm embedding for figure %s', meta.get('image_url')); continue`) so the degradation is observable rather than silent.

title: text.strip() recomputed multiple times in browser-fallback comparison
description: `text.strip()` is recomputed at line 1110 (`len(text.strip()) < _BROWSER_FALLBACK_MIN_CHARS`) and again at line 1128 (`len(rendered_text.strip()) > len(text.strip())`), plus once more at line 1205. For large pages this allocates a fresh stripped copy of the full extracted text each time. Trivial cost relative to the surrounding network/LLM work, hence info-level. Not blindly auto-fixable: `text` is reassigned at line 1129, so a single cached variable cannot span the reassignment — caching must be scoped per comparison.
suggested_fix: Cache the stripped length where it is reused within a single scope (e.g. `cur_len = len(text.strip())` before the comparison at 1128, reusing it in the `< _BROWSER_FALLBACK_MIN_CHARS` guard if restructured). Low value; optional.

title: SSRF: redirect-following reaches internal hosts before post-redirect validation
description: ingest_url validates the initial hostname against is_private_ip (web.py:1068-1078), but then calls httpx.get(url, follow_redirects=True, timeout=30.0) at web.py:1085. The post-redirect check at web.py:1091-1095 only blocks *processing* of the response; httpx has already followed every redirect hop and issued the actual HTTP request to whatever internal address a redirect pointed at. A public URL that 30x-redirects to http://169.254.169.254/ (cloud metadata) or http://127.0.0.1:port/ will have the request reach that internal endpoint, triggering any side effects (GET-as-action endpoints, SSRF-readable metadata) before the response host is rejected. The same pattern exists for inline image fetches at web.py:411-413 (httpx.stream with follow_redirects=True), with its post-redirect check at web.py:418. The code comment at web.py:1080-1083 explicitly acknowledges this gap and tracks the fix in issue #232. Being known-and-tracked does not remove it from an audit.
suggested_fix: Implement per-hop SSRF validation. Disable automatic redirect following (follow_redirects=False) and manually walk redirects, running is_private_ip on each Location host before issuing the next request; or install a custom httpx transport that resolves the hostname, validates the resolved IP, and connects to that pinned IP. Apply the same per-hop guard to the image httpx.stream call at web.py:411-413.

title: SSRF: DNS-rebinding TOCTOU between is_private_ip check and httpx fetch
description: is_private_ip (utils.py:33-55) resolves the hostname via socket.getaddrinfo and rejects if any resolved address is non-global. httpx (web.py:1085, web.py:411-413) then performs its own independent DNS resolution at fetch time. Because the two resolutions are decoupled in time, an attacker controlling the authoritative DNS for a hostname can return a public IP during the validation lookup and a private/loopback IP (e.g. 127.0.0.1 or 169.254.169.254) during the fetch lookup. The 'resolve ALL IPs' logic in utils.py:47-53 defends against multi-homed hosts returning a mix of public/private addresses simultaneously, but not against time-of-check/time-of-use rebinding. This is a classic SSRF bypass of hostname allowlisting.
suggested_fix: Resolve the hostname once, validate the resolved IP, then connect to that pinned IP for the actual request (custom httpx transport or resolver) so the IP used for validation is the same one used for the connection. A short DNS cache TTL pinning per request closes the window.

title: Path traversal: subprocess elements.json filename joined to tmpdir without containment check
description: _render_with_browser reads elements.json produced by the Playwright subprocess, then for each entry builds png_path = tmpdir / entry['file'] (web.py:719) with no validation that the resolved path stays inside tmpdir. A 'file' value such as '../../etc/passwd' or an absolute path would resolve outside the temp directory (pathlib's / operator discards the left operand on an absolute right operand). _extract_element_captures (web.py:920-925) then read_bytes() the path and base64-encodes it to a remote vision endpoint (_vision_call) — an exfiltration-shaped consumer. The producer (render_page.py:46) currently emits only safe 'element_N.png' names, so the trust boundary is our own subprocess and the practical exploit is narrow; but this is a defense-in-depth gap that becomes live if the subprocess output is ever tampered with or the producer changes.
suggested_fix: After building png_path, enforce containment: resolved = (tmpdir / entry['file']).resolve(); reject unless resolved.is_relative_to(tmpdir.resolve()). Alternatively restrict entry['file'] to a strict basename allowlist (e.g. re.fullmatch(r'element_\d+\.png', entry['file'])).

title: Type confusion: elements.json fields consumed without isinstance validation
description: The loop at web.py:716-728 reads entry['file'], entry['tag'], entry.get('width', 0), entry.get('height', 0) from JSON decoded out of the subprocess elements.json with no type validation. If 'file' were a non-string, the Path join would raise; if 'tag' were a dict/list it flows into an f-string prompt (web.py:927) and into stored metadata. This is partially mitigated by the surrounding except (json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError, TypeError) at web.py:729, which catches the most likely failures, and by the producer being trusted code. Flagged as info for completeness at the JSON trust boundary.
suggested_fix: Validate decoded entries before use: ensure raw is a list, each entry is a dict, entry['file'] and entry['tag'] are str, and width/height coerce to int. Skip malformed entries instead of relying on a broad except.

title: SSRF via in-browser page JavaScript (documented accepted trade-off)
description: render_page.py launches Playwright and navigates to the target URL (render_page.py:94). The scheme is re-validated as defense-in-depth (render_page.py:74-78), but once the page loads, page JavaScript can issue arbitrary secondary requests (fetch/XHR/img/iframe) to internal networks (e.g. cloud metadata, intranet services). is_private_ip protects the top-level navigation host but cannot constrain JS-initiated subrequests. The module docstring at render_page.py:3-9 explicitly names this as an accepted trade-off for an optional, user-configured feature, recommending network-level isolation (Docker network, firewall) as mitigation. Reported at info to reflect the documented stance honestly.
suggested_fix: Keep the documented stance. For stronger isolation, run the browser subprocess in a network-segregated container/namespace with egress filtering, or set a Playwright route handler (page.route) that aborts requests resolving to non-global IPs. No source change required if network-level controls are deployed as documented.

title: _extract_html_images is a ~215-LOC function mixing 5 responsibilities
description:
Current state: `_extract_html_images` (lines 329-543, ~215 body LOC) parses candidates, merges extra HTML sources with dedup, downloads+size-guards+SSRF-rechecks each image, decodes via Pillow, calls vision per image, embeds, deletes stale chunks, and inserts new chunks with hash dedup — all in one function body. It far exceeds the 50-LOC threshold and operates at four different abstraction levels (byte streaming, image decoding, DB cleanup, embedding).
Target state: Extract cohesive helpers: `_download_image_bytes(url) -> bytes | None` (the httpx.stream + byte-cap + post-redirect SSRF block, lines 409-447), `_decode_image_to_png_b64(bytes) -> str | None` (lines 450-461), `_describe_image(b64, alt) -> list[(desc, meta)]` (lines 463-489), and a shared `_swap_figure_chunks(conn, source_url, collected, index_start, index_end)` that owns the delete-stale + embed + insert-with-dedup tail (lines 494-543). The top-level function then orchestrates at one altitude.
Pattern name: Extract Method / Compose Method.
Motivation: Testability (each fallible stage independently mockable), readability, and it exposes the duplication shared with `_extract_web_figures`/`_extract_element_captures`.
Estimated scope: 1 file, ~6 new private helpers, plus test updates.
Prerequisites: none.
Risk: Medium — the embed-before-delete ordering (lines 494-516) is a deliberate crash-safety invariant; the extraction must preserve 'delete stale only after embeddings succeed' or it silently corrupts idempotency.
suggested_fix: Decompose into _download_image_bytes / _decode_image_to_png_b64 / _describe_image / _swap_figure_chunks helpers; keep the embed-then-delete ordering intact in the shared swap helper.

title: Three figure-extraction functions duplicate the embed/delete-stale/insert-dedup tail
description:
Current state: `_extract_html_images` (494-543), `_extract_web_figures` (829-881), and `_extract_element_captures` (969-1012) each repeat the identical pipeline: build `texts` list, call `_embed_with_config`, query stale `chunk_index` range for `source_type='figure'`, call `_cleanup_figure_fk_refs` + delete (via `delete_chunks_cascade` in one, `delete_chunk_vecs`+`_batched_execute` in the other two — an inconsistency, see separate finding), then loop `zip(collected, embeddings)` computing `_content_hash`, skip-if-exists, `_insert_chunk`, increment counter, `conn.commit()` if changed. Each also opens with `import base64` + `from .vision import _get_vision_config` + reads `base_url`/`model`.
Target state: A single `_swap_figure_chunks(conn, source_url, collected, *, index_start, index_end=None)` that takes `collected: list[tuple[str, dict, int]]` (desc, meta, index-offset) and performs embed → scoped-delete → hash-dedup insert → commit. The three callers reduce to per-source collection logic only.
Pattern name: Form Template Method / Extract Superclass (here: extract shared procedure).
Motivation: ~120 lines of near-identical logic; the chunk-index-range scoping is subtle and currently copy-pasted, so a fix to one (e.g. the delete-API inconsistency) must be remembered in three places.
Estimated scope: 1 file, 1 new helper, 3 call-site rewrites, test consolidation.
Prerequisites: reconcile the delete-API inconsistency (delete_chunks_cascade vs delete_chunk_vecs+manual DELETE) first.
Risk: Medium — the index ranges differ per caller and encode an ordering contract between the three figure sources; must be parameterized exactly.
suggested_fix: Extract a parameterized _swap_figure_chunks(conn, source_url, collected, index_start, index_end) and route all three figure extractors through it.

title: Stale-chunk deletion uses two different APIs across the three figure extractors
description: `_extract_html_images` deletes stale chunks via `delete_chunks_cascade(conn, old_ids)` (line 515), while `_extract_web_figures` (lines 843-844) and `_extract_element_captures` (lines 984-985) instead call `delete_chunk_vecs(conn, ...)` followed by a manual `_batched_execute(conn, "DELETE FROM chunks WHERE id IN ({ph})", ...)`. All three claim to do the same thing (remove stale figure chunks + their vecs). Either `delete_chunks_cascade` is the correct single entry point and the other two are an incomplete reimplementation (risking missed cascade tables), or the manual form is intentional and the names are misleading. This is a latent maintainability/correctness hazard: a future cascade-table addition will be picked up by one path and silently missed by the other two.
suggested_fix: Confirm delete_chunks_cascade is the canonical helper; replace the two delete_chunk_vecs+manual-DELETE sites with it (or document why a vec-only delete differs). Verify the cascade covers the same FK tables _cleanup_figure_fk_refs handles.

title: ingest_url is a ~200-LOC procedure conflating fetch, browser fallback, figure orchestration, and text chunking
description:
Current state: `ingest_url` (1058-1260, ~200 body LOC) does URL validation+SSRF, httpx fetch, post-redirect SSRF, trafilatura extract, the entire browser-fallback block (1110-1176: render, re-extract, screenshot figures, element captures, tmpdir cleanup in a nested try/finally), inline-image extraction, then text-chunk dedup+embed+insert. The browser-fallback block alone is ~67 lines nested 4 levels deep.
Target state: Extract `_fetch_and_validate(url) -> (html, response)`, `_maybe_browser_fallback(conn, url, response, text, extracted_title) -> BrowserOutcome` (returns figures_extracted, browser_rendered, rendered_html, rendered_base_url, possibly-improved text/title), and `_ingest_text_chunks(conn, text, url, title, session_id) -> dict`. `ingest_url` becomes a ~30-line orchestrator.
Pattern name: Extract Method / Compose Method; introduce a small result dataclass for the browser outcome.
Motivation: The nested try/finally with tmpdir lifecycle (1173-1176) and the 'unconditional rendered_html capture even when text not better' subtlety (1132-1143) are easy to break; isolating them makes the invariant explicit and testable.
Estimated scope: 1 file, 3 helpers + 1 dataclass, test updates.
Prerequisites: dataclass for browser outcome (see primitive-obsession finding).
Risk: Medium — the control flow that mutates `text`/`extracted_title`/`browser_rendered` conditionally must be preserved exactly.
suggested_fix: Split ingest_url into _fetch_and_validate, _maybe_browser_fallback (returning a BrowserOutcome dataclass), and _ingest_text_chunks; reduce ingest_url to orchestration.

title: Untyped dict returns used as informal structs where dataclasses belong
description: `_get_browser_config` (560-582) returns `dict | None` with conventional keys `mode`/`venv`/`endpoint`; `_render_with_browser` (654-738) returns a `dict | None` with keys `html`/`screenshot_path`/`final_url`/`element_captures`/`tmpdir`; callers then index by string (`render_result["html"]`, `.get("final_url")`, `browser_config.get("mode")`). These are stable, fixed-shape records — classic primitive obsession. A `@dataclass(frozen=True) BrowserConfig` and `RenderResult` would give the type checker the field set, prevent typo-keys, document the contract, and make the `tmpdir` ownership explicit (it could expose a context manager / `__enter__`/`__exit__`). The element-capture dicts (`{path,tag,width,height}`, lines 720-727) and vision-config dicts are the same pattern.
suggested_fix: Introduce frozen dataclasses BrowserConfig(mode, venv, endpoint) and RenderResult(html, screenshot_path, final_url, element_captures, tmpdir); return those instead of bare dicts. Optionally make RenderResult a context manager owning tmpdir cleanup.

title: tmpdir lifecycle leaks across the function boundary instead of using a context manager
description: `_render_with_browser` creates `tmpdir = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp(...))` (line 665), cleans it on every internal failure path (672, 693, 701) but on success returns it inside the result dict and documents 'Caller owns tmpdir cleanup on success' (663-664). The caller `ingest_url` must remember to `shutil.rmtree(tmpdir, ignore_errors=True)` in a finally (1173-1176). Split ownership across a function boundary is the exact problem context managers solve. A leak occurs if any future caller forgets the finally. Pythonic fix: make rendering a `@contextlib.contextmanager` (`with _browser_render(url, cfg) as result:`) so cleanup is guaranteed by the `with` block regardless of caller discipline.
suggested_fix: Convert _render_with_browser into a contextmanager yielding a RenderResult and rmtree-ing the tmpdir on exit; callers use `with`. Removes the manual finally in ingest_url.

title: figure_type / source_type / original_source_type are bare string literals, not an Enum
description: Metadata records repeat string-literal type tags: `source_type="figure"` (passed to _insert_chunk in all three extractors), `figure_type: "web_image"` (482), `figure_type: fig.get("figure_type", "web_screenshot")` (858), `"original_source_type": "web"` (485, 861, 960), and `source_type='figure'` appears in 4 raw SQL strings (505, 836, 977, 1035). These are a closed vocabulary scattered as magic strings; a typo (`"figrue"`) is silent. `enum.StrEnum` (Python 3.11+, project targets 3.12) for SourceType / FigureType would centralize the vocabulary, give the type checker the value set, and keep SQL/metadata in sync. The duplicated dimension/chunk-index constants are already well-named module constants — this is the remaining un-named-constant gap.
suggested_fix: Define class SourceType(StrEnum) and class FigureType(StrEnum) (or module constants) and reference them in metadata dicts and SQL parameter values instead of raw literals.

title: Function-local imports of .vision in hot paths mask a circular dependency
description: Three functions perform module-level work via function-local imports: `_extract_html_images` does `import base64; import io; from PIL import Image; from .vision import _get_vision_config, _vision_call` inside the body (357-362); `_extract_web_figures` imports six names from `.vision` locally (763-770); `_extract_element_captures` likewise (902-904). This is a deliberate circular-import workaround — `vision.py:31` imports `from .web import _cleanup_figure_fk_refs` (confirmed), so web<->vision is a cycle. Local imports hide the contract and re-run import machinery on every call (cheap after first, but obscures dependencies from static analysis and tooling). The real fix is to break the cycle: `_cleanup_figure_fk_refs` is a DB-figure-cleanup helper that arguably belongs in db.py or a shared figures.py, letting both web.py and vision.py import it top-level.
suggested_fix: Relocate _cleanup_figure_fk_refs to a neutral module (db.py or a new figures.py) so web.py and vision.py both import it top-level, breaking the cycle; then hoist the .vision and PIL/base64/io imports to module scope.

title: for/else with a no-op `pass` body adds noise
description: In `_extract_html_images` the streaming loop has `else: # Loop completed without break — download OK \n pass` (lines 437-439). The for/else does nothing — the `total > _MAX_IMAGE_DOWNLOAD_BYTES` check immediately after (line 441) already handles both the break and no-break cases. The empty else clause is dead structure that misleads the reader into thinking the for/else carries semantics. Delete it.
suggested_fix: Remove the `else: pass` clause; the subsequent `if total > _MAX_IMAGE_DOWNLOAD_BYTES: continue` already covers the over-size case.

title: Browser timeouts and screenshot clip dimensions are unnamed magic numbers
description: `render_page.py` defines named constants for element capture (`_MIN_ELEMENT_DIMENSION = 80`, `_MAX_ELEMENT_CAPTURES = 10`, lines 20-21) but leaves the main-flow tunables as inline literals: `timeout=30_000` (line 94, page.goto), `page.wait_for_timeout(2_000)` (96, JS settle), and the screenshot clip `{"width": 1280, "height": 8000}` (107). The 8000-height cap is even commented as a memory bound but not named. These are exactly the kind of operational knobs that should be named module constants (`_GOTO_TIMEOUT_MS`, `_JS_SETTLE_MS`, `_SCREENSHOT_WIDTH`, `_SCREENSHOT_MAX_HEIGHT`) for discoverability and single-point tuning, matching the style already used a few lines above.
suggested_fix: Promote 30_000 / 2_000 / 1280 / 8000 to named module constants alongside the existing _MIN_ELEMENT_DIMENSION / _MAX_ELEMENT_CAPTURES.

title: _capture_elements page param is untyped with a blanket no-untyped-def suppression
description: `def _capture_elements(page, out_dir: Path) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]` (line 24) suppresses the type checker for the whole signature rather than typing `page`. Playwright ships `playwright.sync_api.Page`; importing it under `TYPE_CHECKING` (to avoid the runtime import cost on the non-browser path) would let the parameter be annotated `page: Page` and remove the blanket ignore. The blanket `no-untyped-def` ignore also silences any future signature drift on this function.
suggested_fix: Add `if TYPE_CHECKING: from playwright.sync_api import Page` and annotate `page: Page`, dropping the `# type: ignore[no-untyped-def]`.

title: Bare `except Exception: continue` swallows all errors during element capture
description: `_capture_elements` wraps `el.bounding_box()` (32-34) and `el.screenshot(...)` (47-50) in `except Exception: continue`. While fail-soft is reasonable for a best-effort capture loop, swallowing every Exception with no logging makes diagnosing 'why did my canvas not get captured' impossible — there is no stderr breadcrumb, unlike the caller side in web.py which uses `logger.warning(..., exc_info=True)`. Since this is a subprocess whose stderr is captured (`capture_output=True` at web.py:684), a `print(..., file=sys.stderr)` per swallowed error would surface in the parent's logging without changing the fail-soft behavior.
suggested_fix: Narrow to the expected Playwright error types or add a `print(f"skip element: {exc}", file=sys.stderr)` in the except blocks so failures are observable in the captured subprocess stderr.

title: browser/render_page.py has zero test coverage
description: The entire render_page.py module is untested. `_capture_elements` (lines 24-62) and `main` (lines 65-116) have no test imports or calls anywhere in tests/. The only `test_render_page_*` tests (tests/test_vision.py:364-379) target `knowledge_base.vision._render_page` (PDF rasterization) — an unrelated function with a colliding name, NOT this browser module. Untested logic includes: the canvas/svg element selection, the `_MIN_ELEMENT_DIMENSION`/`_MAX_ELEMENT_CAPTURES` (80px / 10-cap) filters, the viewport-bounds rejection (`box['x']+w <= 0`), the bounding_box/screenshot try/except continue paths, the manifest write-only-if-nonempty branch (line 61), and `main`'s non-http(s) scheme rejection + sys.exit(1) (lines 76-78). Playwright is the hard dependency, but `_capture_elements` takes a `page` object and can be driven with a Mock exposing `query_selector_all`, and `main`'s scheme guard runs before the playwright import (line 80) so it is unit-testable without playwright.
suggested_fix: Add tests/test_render_page.py. (1) `_capture_elements`: feed a fake `page` whose `query_selector_all` returns Mock elements with controllable `bounding_box()`, `evaluate()`, and `screenshot()`; assert filtering by min dimension, the 10-element cap, viewport-bounds rejection, the bounding_box-raises-skip path, and that elements.json is only written when at least one capture survives. (2) `main` scheme guard: invoke with `sys.argv` set to a `ftp://` URL via monkeypatch and assert `SystemExit(1)` is raised before the playwright import — this needs no browser.

title: ingest_url post-redirect SSRF rejection has no test
description: ingest_url's post-redirect SSRF guard (lines 1091-1095: raise ValidationError 'URL redirected to a private/internal address') is never asserted. grep across tests/ finds no test that drives `ingest_url` with a `response.url` whose hostname is a private IP. The existing redirect-to-private test (tests/test_ingest.py:2300 `test_extract_html_images_rejects_redirect_to_private`) exercises the image-download path in `_extract_html_images`, not the page-fetch guard in `ingest_url`. This is a security-relevant branch (SSRF defense-in-depth) left uncovered.
suggested_fix: Add a test that patches `knowledge_base.web.httpx.get` to return a response whose `.url` is e.g. `http://169.254.169.254/` (or `http://127.0.0.1/`) while the requested URL is public, and assert `pytest.raises(ValidationError, match='redirected to a private')`. Patch `is_private_ip` if needed to make the final-host check deterministic.

title: _render_with_browser malformed elements.json branch untested
description: In `_render_with_browser`, the elements.json parsing block (lines 716-730) has an except clause for `(json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError, TypeError)` that logs a warning and continues with empty element_captures. The happy path is covered by tests/test_ingest.py:3787 (`test_render_with_browser_produces_element_captures`), but the malformed-JSON / missing-key fallback is never exercised. No test writes an invalid or schema-incomplete elements.json. The result['element_captures'] == [] degradation path is unverified.
suggested_fix: Add a test variant of test_render_with_browser_produces_element_captures whose `_fake_run` writes `elements.json` containing invalid JSON (e.g. '{not json') and another writing valid JSON missing the required 'file'/'tag' keys. Assert `_render_with_browser` still returns a dict with `element_captures == []` and does not raise.

title: _find_venv_python has no direct test; Windows branch uncovered
description: `_find_venv_python` (lines 551-557) is only ever mocked out (tests/test_ingest.py:3805) or covered indirectly via `_make_fake_venv` which always creates a POSIX `bin/python` layout. The Windows `Scripts/python.exe` candidate branch (line 554) and the None-return-when-missing path are never directly asserted. Cross-platform venv discovery is exactly the kind of logic that silently breaks on the untested OS.
suggested_fix: Add a small parametrized unit test for `_find_venv_python`: (a) tmp_path with `bin/python` created → returns that path; (b) tmp_path with `Scripts/python.exe` created → returns that path (covers the Windows branch on any OS since it only checks `is_file()`); (c) empty tmp_path → returns None.

title: _cleanup_figure_fk_refs FK-nulling not directly asserted
description: `_cleanup_figure_fk_refs` (lines 220-253) nulls FK references across papers.abstract_chunk_id, relationships.evidence_chunk_id, methods/datasets/metrics.chunk_id, and deletes entity_mentions before a figure chunk is removed. It is invoked transitively by stale-cleanup tests, but no test seeds a figure chunk that is referenced by those FK columns and then asserts the references were actually nulled/deleted (rather than left dangling or blocking deletion). The empty-list early-return (line 222) and the multi-table loop are unverified for correctness. A regression here would corrupt referential integrity silently.
suggested_fix: Add a focused test: insert a figure chunk, point a papers.abstract_chunk_id, a relationships.evidence_chunk_id, a methods.chunk_id, and an entity_mentions row at it, call `_cleanup_figure_fk_refs(conn, [chunk_id])`, then assert each FK is NULL and the entity_mentions row is gone. Also assert the `chunk_ids=[]` path is a no-op.

title: Repeated @patch stacks and inline HTML duplicated across image tests; no shared fixtures
description: The _extract_html_images integration tests (tests/test_ingest.py ~1966-2660) each repeat the same `@patch('knowledge_base.web.is_private_ip', return_value=False)` + `@patch('knowledge_base.web.httpx.stream')` decorator stack and re-mock vision config / build PNG bytes inline. There is no module-level fixture factory for a 'valid PNG response' or a 'vision-configured conn', so each test reconstructs the mock plumbing. This is maintainable but bloats the file and makes the streaming-response mock shape (a context manager with `iter_bytes`) easy to get subtly wrong per-test. A typed fixture factory would reduce duplication and centralize the httpx.stream contract.
suggested_fix: Introduce a `@pytest.fixture` factory (function-scoped) returning a configured connection with vision mocked, and a helper `make_stream_response(png_bytes, final_url=...)` building the httpx.stream context-manager mock. Consider grouping the image-extraction tests under a class with these as fixtures, mirroring the existing element-capture test class at line ~3787.

title: _parse_srcset cases are 10 separate functions instead of one parametrized table
description: `_parse_srcset` has good case coverage (w-descriptors, x-descriptors, baseline tie, svg/data-uri skip, malformed, whitespace, empty, all-svg) but each is a separate one-assertion function (tests/test_ingest.py:1763-1819). This is exactly the combinatorial-input shape `@pytest.mark.parametrize` exists for: a single parametrized test with (srcset, current_src, expected) tuples would be denser, easier to extend, and would surface missing combinations (e.g. mixed w+x descriptor sets, which the dominant-kind logic at lines 130-136 handles but no test exercises).
suggested_fix: Collapse the _parse_srcset_* functions into one `@pytest.mark.parametrize`'d test over (srcset, current_src, expected) tuples, and add the currently-missing mixed-descriptor case (e.g. 'a.jpg 600w, b.jpg 2x') to verify the 'w' kind dominates 'x' per lines 131-133.

title: Public ingest_url docstring documents no params, return, or raises
description: ingest_url is part of the module's public API (listed in __all__ at lines 37-40) yet its docstring (lines 1063-1067) is prose-only: it documents none of its three parameters (conn, url, session_id), no return value, and no exceptions. The signature takes session_id: str | None = None whose effect (co-occurrence/session-link tracking, used at lines 1224-1225, 1247, 1252) is entirely undocumented and non-obvious. The function returns a structured dict (lines 1197-1260 with keys url/source_uri/source_type/browser_rendered/figures_extracted/chunks_added/chunks_skipped and conditionally title) but the return shape is undocumented. It raises ValidationError in five places (lines 1070, 1074, 1076, 1088, 1093) with no Raises section. The MCP-tool reference docs/reference/mcp-tools.md:58-84 carry the parameter table and return shape, but the source docstring lags behind.
suggested_fix: Add a Google-style Args/Returns/Raises block: document conn, url, session_id (noting session_id drives chunk_sessions co-occurrence tracking), describe the returned dict keys, and add Raises: ValidationError for unsupported scheme, missing hostname, private/internal address (pre-fetch and post-redirect), and fetch failure.

title: configure_browser Args block omits conn, has a malformed entry, and lacks Raises
description: configure_browser (public, in __all__) uses a Google-style Args: section (lines 592-597) but it is incomplete and malformed. (1) The conn parameter is not documented. (2) The line 'Pass both as empty string to disable. Both None to query.' (line 597) is indented at the same level as the parameter names, so it parses as a malformed Args entry without a parameter name rather than a general note. (3) The function raises ValidationError in four distinct places (line 612 CDP-without-venv, line 620 non-absolute venv_path, line 623 missing Python executable, lines 629-631 bad CDP scheme) but there is no Raises: section. (4) The function returns a dict (lines 601, 608, 651) but there is no Returns: documentation.
suggested_fix: Document conn in Args; move the 'Pass both as empty string / Both None' sentence out of the Args block into the summary or a Notes line at the correct indentation; add a Raises: ValidationError clause covering the four conditions; add a Returns: line describing the {'browser': ...} dict.

title: _render_with_browser return docstring is stale (missing final_url and element_captures)
description: The docstring states the success return is `{"html": str, "screenshot_path": Path, "tmpdir": Path}` (lines 661-662), but the actual returned dict (lines 732-738) contains five keys: html, screenshot_path, final_url, element_captures, and tmpdir. The two extra keys (final_url at line 736, element_captures at line 737) were added later (Phase 3 #132 element captures; final_url handling) and the docstring was not updated. Additionally the docstring types screenshot_path as Path, but line 734 returns `screenshot_path if screenshot_path.exists() else None`, so the value can be None.
suggested_fix: Update the docstring return description to list all five keys and correct the screenshot_path type to Path | None: e.g. `{"html": str, "screenshot_path": Path | None, "final_url": str | None, "element_captures": list[dict], "tmpdir": Path}`.

title: _get_browser_config docstring overstates the returned dict keys
description: The docstring says it 'Returns a dict with mode/endpoint/venv keys' (line 562), but the endpoint key is only present in CDP mode (added conditionally at lines 577-580). In local mode the returned dict contains only mode and venv (line 576). A reader relying on the docstring may assume endpoint is always present.
suggested_fix: Reword to clarify endpoint is conditional, e.g. 'Returns a dict with mode and venv keys, plus endpoint when mode is cdp; or None when unconfigured.'

title: Module docstring singles out CDP for ephemeral context, but both modes use it
description: The module security-notes docstring states 'CDP mode uses ephemeral browser context (no shared state).' (line 6), implying ephemerality is CDP-specific. In practice browser.new_context() (line 91) creates a fresh ephemeral context for both CDP and local-launch modes; the inline comment at line 90 ('Ephemeral context: no shared cookies/cache/service-workers') applies unconditionally. The docstring wording could mislead a reader into thinking local mode shares state.
suggested_fix: Generalize the note, e.g. 'Both CDP and local modes use an ephemeral browser context (no shared cookies/cache/service-workers).'

title: Exception in except-tuple makes httpx types redundant and swallows all errors
description: `except (httpx.ConnectError, httpx.TimeoutException, Exception)` includes the base class `Exception` alongside two of its subclasses. The two httpx entries are dead (subsumed by `Exception`), and the handler actually catches *every* exception — including programming errors (AttributeError, TypeError from a malformed response, KeyError) and even unrelated bugs in `resp.json()`. The function then silently returns False, masking real defects as 'server unhealthy'. The narrowed tuple was clearly the intent; `Exception` defeats it. A health check that swallows AttributeError will report a healthy-but-buggy code path as merely 'down', hiding the true failure.
suggested_fix: Drop the bare `Exception` and catch only what a health probe legitimately tolerates, e.g. `except (httpx.HTTPError, json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError):`. httpx.ConnectError/TimeoutException are subclasses of httpx.HTTPError, so the single base covers the network cases; add json.JSONDecodeError for `resp.json()`. Let genuine programming errors propagate.

title: _validate_figure passes through entities_mentioned and title without type checking
description: `_validate_figure` rigorously type-checks `figure_type` and `description` (must be non-empty str) but returns `obj.get('entities_mentioned', [])` and `obj.get('title')` verbatim from untrusted LLM JSON. A vision model can emit `entities_mentioned` as a string, dict, or null, and `title` as a non-str. These values flow into `meta_dict['entities_mentioned']`/`meta_dict['title']` (lines 2022-2023) and are `json.dumps`-ed into chunk metadata — JSON serialization will not catch a wrong *shape* (a str where a list is expected), so the type confusion silently persists into the DB and surfaces only when a downstream consumer iterates entities_mentioned expecting a list.
suggested_fix: Coerce/validate: `entities = obj.get('entities_mentioned', []); entities = entities if isinstance(entities, list) else []` and `title = obj.get('title'); title = title if isinstance(title, str) else None`. Optionally filter entities to str elements.

title: Quadratic caption dedup via list membership inside per-page loop
description: In `_extract_captions`, page-keyed captions are deduplicated with `if cap not in by_page.get(page_0idx, [])` followed by `setdefault(...).append(cap)`. Each `not in` is an O(k) linear scan of the growing per-page list, executed for every (page, caption) pair, giving O(k^2) behaviour per page. For documents with many caption lines accumulated onto the same page (multi-page chunks each contributing the same captions to a shared page), this is needless quadratic work plus repeated `by_page.get`/`setdefault` dict lookups in the inner loop.
suggested_fix: Track a per-page `set` of seen captions (e.g. `seen_by_page: dict[int, set[str]]`) and gate the append on set membership (O(1)), appending to the ordered list only when newly seen. This preserves insertion order while making dedup linear.

title: OmniParser pipeline issues blocking subprocess/HTTP calls strictly serially
description: `_run_omniparser_pipeline` loops over `extracted_images` (line 1653) and `rendered` pages (line 1662) calling `_run_omniparser` one at a time. Each call is a blocking subprocess spawn (up to 120s timeout) or HTTP POST (up to 30s), so total OmniParser latency scales linearly with item count even though the subsequent vision phase already uses a `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)`. For the HTTP-server mode in particular (I/O-bound, GIL released during the request) the calls are trivially parallelizable and would benefit from the same thread-pool fan-out the vision phase uses. Subprocess mode is GPU-bound so concurrency there is a deliberate trade-off, but the HTTP path leaves throughput on the table.
suggested_fix: When `server_url` is set (HTTP mode), dispatch `_run_omniparser` across a small ThreadPoolExecutor (matching the vision pool) and collect via as_completed, keying results by image name / page num. Keep subprocess mode serial (or a low worker count) to avoid GPU oversubscription. Gate concurrency on which mode resolved.

title: Dict-unwrap materializes all values to inspect a single-key case
description: In `_vision_call`, the dict-unwrap path does `values = list(parsed.values())` then checks `len(values) == 1 and isinstance(values[0], list)`. This copies every value of the parsed dict into a list purely to test the single-key shape. Negligible for typical small vision payloads, but it is an unnecessary full materialization on a hot per-call path; `len(parsed) == 1` plus `next(iter(parsed.values()))` avoids the copy.
suggested_fix: Replace with `if len(parsed) == 1: (val,) = parsed.values()` then `if isinstance(val, list): parsed = val else: raise ...`, avoiding the intermediate list. Keep the existing error message using `list(parsed.keys())` only on the error branch.

title: Repeated page_results[page_num] subscript lookups in enrichment loop
description: `_enrich_with_omniparser` indexes `page_results[page_num]` three times per page (lines 1864, 1867 — the latter assigned to `figures_on_page`). The first two (`if not page_results[page_num]` then `figures_on_page = page_results[page_num]`) are redundant dict subscripts; iterating `page_results.items()` once and reusing the bound value removes them. Trivial cost, but it is a hot post-processing loop over all figures.
suggested_fix: Iterate `for page_num, figures_on_page in page_results.items():` and guard with `if not figures_on_page: continue`, reusing the bound list for both the index lookup and the in-place reassignment.

title: OmniParser server_url stored without scheme/host validation, later used in httpx requests
description: configure_omniparser() stores the operator-supplied server_url verbatim into the config table (lines 238-243) with no validation whatsoever. By contrast, configure_vision() validates that base_url uses http/https (lines 133-140). The stored omniparser_server_url is later consumed directly by httpx.get(f"{base_url}/health") in _check_omniparser_server (line 293) and httpx.post(f"{base_url}/parse") in _run_omniparser_http (line 315), and is the value returned by _resolve_omniparser_server_url for the request path (lines 1611-1619). Because the value is operator-configured (local MCP trust model documented at lines 162-167), this is not a remotely-exploitable SSRF, but the asymmetry means a malformed or internal-targeting URL (e.g. file://, http://169.254.169.254/, http://localhost:<other-service>) is accepted silently and issued as an HTTP request with no allowlist. The /v1 stripping and rstrip('/') normalization applied to vision_base_url (line 118) is also not applied to the omniparser URL.
suggested_fix: Mirror the configure_vision validation in configure_omniparser: parse server_url with urllib.parse.urlparse and reject any scheme other than http/https before storing (raise ValidationError). Optionally constrain the host to an explicit allowlist or to loopback/known GPU-node hosts. Apply the same rstrip('/') normalization used for vision_base_url so callers do not double-slash the path.

title: Image basename from chunk metadata joined to image_dir without path normalization
description: In _collect_extracted_images(), img_name values are read from each chunk's JSON metadata 'images' field (line 1282) and joined directly to the on-disk directory as image_dir / img_name (line 1292), then existence-checked and later read via img_path.read_bytes() in _dispatch_vision_calls (line 1729) and _run_omniparser (line 1654). No canonicalization or basename extraction is applied, so a metadata value such as '../../../../etc/passwd' or an absolute path would escape image_dir (Path('/a') / '/etc/passwd' resolves to '/etc/passwd'). This is inconsistent with the caption-extraction path at line 1368, which defensively normalizes with Path(m.group(1)).name. The metadata is produced by the local ingest pipeline (pymupdf4llm basenames), so this is a defense-in-depth gap rather than a request-reachable traversal, but tampered or malformed DB rows would be read/exfiltrated to the vision/OmniParser endpoints. Note: _save_rendered_pngs (lines 2073-2079) is safe because it formats filenames from integer page numbers.
suggested_fix: Normalize before joining: img_name = Path(img_name).name (matching line 1368), and after resolving verify the path stays under image_dir, e.g. resolved = (image_dir / Path(img_name).name).resolve(); if image_dir.resolve() not in resolved.parents: skip. This makes the basename handling consistent across both code paths.

title: God module: 2279 LOC mixing config, server lifecycle, clustering, captions, and orchestration
description: Current state: vision.py is 2279 lines (well past the project's own 1000+ god-module threshold) holding at least six distinct concerns: (a) config read/write (_get_vision_config, configure_vision, _get_omniparser_config, _validate_omniparser_path, configure_omniparser), (b) OmniParser server lifecycle + HTTP (_check_omniparser_server, _run_omniparser_http, _ensure_omniparser_server, _shutdown_omniparser_server, _run_omniparser), (c) geometric clustering/cropping (_cluster_bboxes, _split_cluster_x, _crop_regions, _cluster_drawing_rects, _detect_mixed_page_vector_regions, _elements_in_region), (d) caption extraction (CaptionMap, _extract_captions), (e) prompt building, (f) the multi-stage extraction pipeline (extract_figures and its _resolve/_collect/_render/_dispatch/_enrich/_persist stages). Target state: a vision/ package split into config.py, omniparser_server.py, clustering.py, captions.py, prompts.py, and pipeline.py, with vision/__init__.py re-exporting the four public names. Pattern: Package-by-feature / module extraction. Motivation: a single file of this size is hard to navigate, has high merge-conflict surface, and forces unrelated changes to touch the same file. Estimated scope: HEAVY (~6 new modules, import rewiring across server routes and tests). Prerequisites: stable public API (only 4 names in __all__) makes the boundary clean. Risk: medium — internal imports and test imports (tests reach into private helpers, e.g. _build_figure_vision_prompt) must be repointed; circular-import care around .papers/.ingest/.web.
suggested_fix: Split vision.py into a vision/ package by concern (config, omniparser_server, clustering, captions, prompts, pipeline); keep the 4 public functions re-exported from vision/__init__.py.

title: Module-level mutable singleton for the OmniParser server process
description: Current state: the auto-started server is tracked in two module globals, _omniparser_process and _omniparser_lock (lines 341-342), mutated via 'global _omniparser_process' in _shutdown_omniparser_server (line 350) and _ensure_omniparser_server (lines 384, 459). This is global mutable process state with double-checked locking spread across free functions, plus an atexit handler registered at line 460. Target state: encapsulate the process handle, lock, readiness wait, and shutdown in an OmniParserServerManager class (one instance held module-level, or injected), so lifecycle invariants live in one object and the global/atexit wiring is contained. Pattern: encapsulate global state in a manager/singleton object. Motivation: scattered 'global' writes across functions obscure the lifecycle contract, complicate testing (tests must reach into and reset module globals), and make the double-checked-locking invariant hard to audit. Estimated scope: HEAVY — restructures three functions and the atexit registration. Prerequisites: none. Risk: medium — must preserve the existing double-checked-locking and atexit-cleanup semantics exactly.
suggested_fix: Introduce an OmniParserServerManager class owning the process handle, lock, readiness wait, and terminate/atexit logic; replace the module globals and 'global' statements with instance state.
references: CLAUDE.md: 'module-level mutable singleton state; global mutable state'

title: Primitive obsession: figure represented as a dict with underscore-prefixed sentinel keys
description: Current state: the figure object flows through the pipeline as a plain dict and accumulates side-channel keys by mutation: _source_image (1799), _vision_failed (1819), _region_idx (1830) in _dispatch_vision_calls; _ocr_text via {**fig, '_ocr_text': ...} in _enrich_with_omniparser (1876, 1893); _assembled_content and _caption in _persist_figures (1939-1940). The 'real' keys (figure_type, title, description, entities_mentioned) come from _validate_figure (lines 795-800) which already produces a dict. This is primitive obsession: a structured record modeled as an untyped dict where a dataclass belongs, with the public/transport keys and internal-pipeline keys intermixed and no type checking on the underscore fields. Target state: a frozen-ish Figure dataclass with typed fields (figure_type: str, description: str, title: str | None, entities_mentioned: list[str]) plus an internal-state companion (source_image, vision_failed, region_idx, ocr_text, assembled_content, caption) carried in a PipelineFigure wrapper or dataclasses.replace updates. Pattern: Replace Data Value With Object / Introduce Parameter Object. Motivation: typed fields catch key typos, document the contract, and stop the 'string key as schema' pattern; mypy/pyright can then verify the pipeline. Estimated scope: HEAVY — touches _validate_figure, _dispatch_vision_calls, _enrich_with_omniparser, _persist_figures, and the metadata-building block (2019-2037). Prerequisites: decide whether vision-LLM raw dict is converted at _validate_figure boundary. Risk: medium — the JSON unwrap path and the {**fig, ...} merges must be faithfully ported.
suggested_fix: Introduce a Figure dataclass (typed required fields) and carry pipeline-internal state in dataclass fields or a wrapper instead of underscore-prefixed dict keys; convert at the _validate_figure boundary.

title: _dispatch_vision_calls is ~148 LOC spanning task-build, result-collect, and region-merge
description: Current state: _dispatch_vision_calls (1698-1845) does four things in one body: builds futures for extracted images (1723-1740), for vector-page renders/crops (1742-1771), for mixed-page regions (1773-1788), then collects results with fallback handling (1793-1823) and flattens region maps into per-page lists (1825-1832). ~148 LOC, three submission loops plus collection logic. Target state: split into _submit_extracted_image_tasks, _submit_vector_page_tasks, _submit_mixed_region_tasks (each returning future->key entries) and _collect_vision_results / _merge_region_figures. Pattern: Extract Method / Compose Method. Motivation: the function exceeds the 50-LOC guideline; the three submission shapes share only the executor and obscure each path's distinct key convention (note the _MIXED_REGION_OFFSET trick at 1786). Estimated scope: LIGHT-to-MEDIUM (mechanical extraction within one module). Prerequisites: none. Risk: low — pure decomposition, no behavior change, but care needed that all futures share one executor scope.
suggested_fix: Extract the three task-submission loops and the collect/merge phase into helper functions; the executor block then reads as submit-x3 then collect+merge.

title: _persist_figures is ~159 LOC mixing content assembly, scoped-DELETE SQL, and chunk insertion
description: Current state: _persist_figures (1899-2057) interleaves: caption lookup + content assembly + text collection (1912-1943), batch embedding (1946-1948), construction of the scoped-DELETE subquery with manual OR-clause/param building (1950-1974), cascade cleanup/delete, and the per-figure insert loop including chunk_index encoding, content-hash dedup, enrichment-layer tracking, and metadata dict assembly (1988-2050). ~159 LOC. Target state: extract _build_figure_delete_scope(source_uri, pages) -> (subquery_sql, params), _build_figure_metadata(figure, page_num, model) -> str, and possibly _assemble_persistable_figures(...) for the content step; _persist_figures becomes an orchestrator. Pattern: Extract Method; the SQL builder is a Replace Inline-SQL-Assembly With Helper. Motivation: over 3x the LOC guideline, and the hand-rolled SQL clause building (1953-1974) is the riskiest part to read inside a 159-line function. Estimated scope: MEDIUM. Prerequisites: none. Risk: low-medium — the transaction (try/commit/rollback at 1978-2055) and the embeddings[i] index alignment with all_figures must be preserved.
suggested_fix: Extract the DELETE-scope SQL builder and the per-figure metadata builder into helpers; reduce _persist_figures to assemble -> embed -> delete-scope -> insert-loop -> commit.

title: _ensure_omniparser_server is ~100 LOC combining health-check, spawn, and readiness polling
description: Current state: _ensure_omniparser_server (363-462) performs five distinct steps in one body: fast-path health check, double-checked locking, server-script + venv-python location, Popen spawn, and a readiness sentinel poll loop with early-exit detection (430-454). ~100 LOC. Target state: extract _spawn_omniparser_process(...) and _wait_for_ready_sentinel(proc, deadline) -> bool helpers; the function becomes health-check -> lock -> spawn -> wait -> register. Pattern: Extract Method. Motivation: exceeds 50-LOC guideline and the readiness loop comment (439-442) explains a subtle blocking-readline workaround that deserves its own named function. Estimated scope: LIGHT. Prerequisites: ideally done together with the OmniParserServerManager extraction (vision/design-module-singleton). Risk: low. NOTE: this finding overlaps with the manager-class refactor and could be folded into it.
suggested_fix: Extract _spawn_omniparser_process and _wait_for_ready_sentinel; ideally as methods of the proposed OmniParserServerManager.

title: extract_figures orchestrator is ~141 LOC
description: Current state: extract_figures (2138-2278) is the top-level orchestrator chaining ~11 numbered stages plus the ETA gate, server-health probe (duplicated with estimate_figures_time, see below), and result-summary dict construction (2252-2267) and drift-warning (2269-2276). ~141 LOC. Target state: it is legitimately a pipeline driver, but the server-health probe block (2170-2174) and the result-summary assembly can be extracted, and the duplicated health-probe/omni_per_page logic shared with estimate_figures_time (2110-2127) should be factored into one helper (e.g. _probe_omniparser_health(conn) and _per_page_eta(omniparser_path, server_healthy)). Pattern: Extract Method + Extract Duplicated Logic. Motivation: over the 50-LOC guideline; more importantly the ETA/health logic is duplicated verbatim between extract_figures and estimate_figures_time. Estimated scope: LIGHT. Prerequisites: none. Risk: low.
suggested_fix: Extract _probe_omniparser_health and _per_page_eta helpers shared by extract_figures and estimate_figures_time, and pull result-summary assembly into a helper.

title: _omniparser_lock uses __import__("threading").Lock() instead of a top-level import
description: Current state: line 342 reads `_omniparser_lock = __import__("threading").Lock()`. Using the __import__ builtin inline is non-idiomatic, defeats static import analysis, and reads as an obfuscation. Target state: add `import threading` to the top-level import block (alongside the other stdlib imports at lines 5-17) and write `_omniparser_lock = threading.Lock()`. Pattern: Use Standard Import. Motivation: PEP 8 / readability; tooling (import linters, IDEs) cannot see the dependency through __import__. Estimated scope: trivial. Risk: none.
suggested_fix: Add `import threading` at the top of the module and replace line 342 with `_omniparser_lock = threading.Lock()`.
references: PEP 8: imports at top of module

title: except clause lists specific exceptions then a blanket Exception, swallowing all errors silently
description: Current state: _check_omniparser_server ends with `except (httpx.ConnectError, httpx.TimeoutException, Exception): return False` (lines 302-303). Because Exception subsumes the two named types, the specific names are dead, and any error (ValueError, AttributeError, JSONDecodeError, a programming bug in this function, etc.) is silently mapped to False with no log line. This is LBYL-style defensive swallowing that hides bugs. Target state: either narrow to the specific expected exceptions plus a logged broad fallback, e.g. catch (httpx.HTTPError, json.JSONDecodeError) and on a separate `except Exception as exc: logger.debug(...)`; at minimum log when the blanket branch fires. Pattern: Narrow/Log Exception Handling. Motivation: silent broad catches are a known maintainability hazard (CLAUDE.md EAFP guidance and 'evidence before assertions'); a health check that returns False for an internal bug masks the real failure. Estimated scope: trivial. Risk: low — behavior (return False on failure) is preserved; only adds visibility / removes redundancy.
suggested_fix: Drop the redundant `Exception` (catch only the httpx errors and json.JSONDecodeError), or keep a final `except Exception as exc:` that logs at debug/warning before returning False.
references: redundant exception in tuple after Exception

title: Magic numbers: 120s ETA-confirmation threshold, 120s startup deadline, 10s shutdown wait
description: Current state: the module defines a clean block of named timing constants (lines 258-266) but three behavioral numbers are still inline literals: the ETA-confirmation gate `if estimated > 120 and not confirmed` (line 1556), the server-startup readiness deadline `time.monotonic() + 120` (line 427), and the terminate-then-kill wait `proc.wait(timeout=10)` (line 356). The literal 120 at line 1556 is the same value as _VISION_CALL_TIMEOUT and _OMNIPARSER_SUBPROCESS_TIMEOUT but means something different (a UX confirm gate), so reusing those would be wrong — it needs its own name. Target state: add _ETA_CONFIRMATION_THRESHOLD_SECS = 120, _OMNIPARSER_STARTUP_DEADLINE_SECS = 120, _OMNIPARSER_SHUTDOWN_WAIT_SECS = 10 in the constants block. Pattern: Replace Magic Number With Named Constant. Motivation: consistency with the existing constants block; intent clarity (the three 120s are coincidentally equal but semantically distinct). Estimated scope: trivial. Risk: none.
suggested_fix: Add named constants for the 120s confirm threshold, the 120s startup deadline, and the 10s shutdown wait, and reference them at lines 1556, 427, 356.

title: figure_type is a free string with an enumerated domain (diagram|chart|table|photo|equation)
description: Current state: figure_type has a fixed enumerated domain advertised in both prompts ('diagram|chart|table|photo|equation' at lines 916 and 949) but is validated only as a non-empty str in _validate_figure (lines 787, 795) and stored as-is in metadata (line 2021). There is no enum and no membership check, so an LLM typo ('digram') or a novel value passes through silently. Target state: define a `class FigureType(enum.StrEnum)` with the five members and validate/normalize against it in _validate_figure (accepting unknown -> a fallback 'unknown' member, since the value is LLM-produced and cannot be trusted to match). Pattern: Replace Type Code With Enum (StrEnum to keep JSON/DB interop). Motivation: CLAUDE.md prefers enum.Enum over string constants; centralizes the canonical list currently duplicated across two prompt strings. Estimated scope: LIGHT but touches validation semantics. Risk: low — must keep serialization as the plain string for DB/metadata compatibility, hence StrEnum; treat as enhancement, not a behavior change.
suggested_fix: Introduce FigureType(enum.StrEnum) with the five canonical values; validate/normalize figure_type in _validate_figure and reference the enum from the prompt builders to remove the duplicated literal list.

title: Function-local imports for urllib, PIL.Image, and atexit
description: Current state: several imports are inside function bodies: `from urllib.parse import urlparse` in configure_vision (line 134), `import io` + `from PIL import Image` in _crop_regions (lines 663-664), and `import atexit` in _ensure_omniparser_server (line 375). None of these appear to be circular-import guards — urllib and atexit are stdlib, PIL is a hard dependency. Function-local imports re-run the import-machinery lookup on every call and hide the module's true dependency surface. Target state: hoist urllib.parse, io, PIL.Image, and atexit to the top-level import block. Pattern: Hoist Import. Motivation: PEP 8 (imports at top), clearer dependency surface, marginal per-call cost removed. Note: the `from .papers import get_paper_source_uri` in _get_paper_source_uri (line 1045) is a likely deliberate circular-import guard (vision <-> papers) and should NOT be hoisted without checking the import graph. Estimated scope: trivial for the four stdlib/PIL imports. Risk: low — confirm PIL is always installed (it is a declared dependency) before hoisting.
suggested_fix: Hoist urllib.parse.urlparse, io, PIL.Image, and atexit to the module top; leave the .papers import local (circular-import guard).
references: PEP 8: imports at top of module

title: _FIGURE_VISION_PROMPT module constant is unused in production (only pinned by a test)
description: Current state: lines 962-963 compute two prompt aliases at import time: `_VISION_PROMPT = _build_page_vision_prompt()` and `_FIGURE_VISION_PROMPT = _build_figure_vision_prompt()`, under a comment 'Backward-compatible aliases for no-caption calls'. Grep confirms _VISION_PROMPT is used in production (line 1780, mixed-page regions), but _FIGURE_VISION_PROMPT is referenced ONLY by tests/test_vision.py:3116-3119 which assert it equals the no-caption build. So _FIGURE_VISION_PROMPT is production-dead: a module-level constant kept alive solely by an equality test. Target state: remove _FIGURE_VISION_PROMPT (and update the test to assert against _build_figure_vision_prompt() directly), or, if the alias is intended as a public stable surface, document why and add it to a tested code path. Pattern: Remove Dead Code. Motivation: dead module-level constant adds import-time work and false signal that it is part of the contract; the 'backward-compatible' comment hints at vestigial wiring. Estimated scope: LIGHT (delete + adjust one test). Risk: low — verify no external/test reliance beyond the one assertion before removal. _VISION_PROMPT is NOT dead and must stay.
suggested_fix: Remove _FIGURE_VISION_PROMPT and change tests/test_vision.py to compare against _build_figure_vision_prompt(); keep _VISION_PROMPT (used at line 1780).

title: DualPathInputs/OmniParserResults are mutable dataclasses though used as immutable value carriers
description: Current state: PaperContext is correctly @dataclass(frozen=True) (line 54), but DualPathInputs (line 64), OmniParserResults (line 75), VisionResults (line 90), and CaptionMap (line 1313) are plain mutable dataclasses. They are constructed once and read-only thereafter (e.g. DualPathInputs in _collect_dual_path_inputs, VisionResults in _dispatch_vision_calls). Their field types embed deeply nested tuple/dict structural types (e.g. lines 79-86) that would be clearer as named type aliases. Target state: mark the result/value dataclasses frozen=True where they are not mutated, and extract the repeated `tuple[dict | None, list[tuple[float,float,float,float]], list[bytes]]` shape into a named alias (e.g. OmniPageEntry). Pattern: Make Value Object Immutable + Introduce Type Alias. Motivation: frozen dataclasses document intent and prevent accidental mutation; the duplicated nested generic (appears at lines 81-86, 1633-1640, 1702-1705) is hard to read. Estimated scope: LIGHT. Risk: low — confirm none of these are mutated in place (CaptionMap.by_page is built incrementally before construction, so it is fine post-construction).
suggested_fix: Add frozen=True to the read-only result dataclasses and introduce a type alias for the repeated OmniParser page-entry tuple shape.

title: _save_rendered_pngs writes to real ~/.local/share, never isolated in tests
description: _save_rendered_pngs() unconditionally builds its output dir from Path.home() / '.local/share/knowledge-base/figures/<paper_id>' and writes PNG bytes there. It is called at the tail of extract_figures() (vision.py:2245) and is NEVER patched, and Path.home()/$HOME is never redirected via monkeypatch in tests/test_vision.py (grep for '_save_rendered_pngs|home|HOME|figures' returns no isolation). Any end-to-end test that exercises the vector-page render path (e.g. test_extract_figures_falls_back_to_render_for_vector_pages, test_extract_figures_processes_mixed_page_vector_regions) writes real files into the developer's/CI's actual home directory, outside tmp_path. Consequences: cross-run pollution keyed by paper_id, CI artifact leakage, and a latent test failure if $HOME is read-only. This is a test-isolation defect, not merely a coverage gap. Fix requires making the figures base dir injectable (parameter or env lookup) in production code so tests can point it at tmp_path; mark as not auto-fixable because it touches the production signature plus the call site plus existing tests.
suggested_fix: Refactor _save_rendered_pngs to accept an optional base_dir (or read a KB data-dir resolved once, defaulting to Path.home()/...), then in tests pass tmp_path (or monkeypatch the data-dir resolver). Add an assertion-bearing test: run extract_figures over a vector-only PDF with the figures dir redirected to tmp_path, assert page_<n>.png and page_<n>_vector_<i>.png are written there and that nothing is created under the real home. Also add an OSError-on-mkdir test to cover the except OSError warning branch at vision.py:2081.

title: Pure clustering/crop helpers have zero direct tests (_cluster_bboxes, _split_cluster_x, _crop_regions, _elements_in_region)
description: Four deterministic, branch-heavy geometry helpers have no direct test references in either tests/test_vision.py or tests/test_vision_omniparser_integration.py (grep for each name returns nothing). _cluster_bboxes (vision.py:555) does Y-gap then X-gap splitting with single-region fallbacks at len<2 (line 584) and post-split len<2 (line 608); _split_cluster_x (vision.py:623) handles the <2 short-circuit and gap splitting; _crop_regions (vision.py:645) does ratio->pixel conversion, padding, and clamping to image bounds; _elements_in_region (vision.py:693) filters by bbox-center containment. These run only when OmniParser returns >1 element (vision.py:1678-1690), so the dense end-to-end suite exercises them only probabilistically/under mocking, not over their branch space. They are ideal parametrize/hypothesis targets: deterministic, no I/O, no DB, no network.
suggested_fix: Add direct parametrized unit tests: (a) _cluster_bboxes with 0/1 bbox -> [(0,0,1,1)], two vertically-separated bboxes (gap>=threshold) -> 2 regions, two close bboxes -> 1 region, malformed bbox (len!=4 or missing) skipped, x1>x2/y1>y2 normalization; (b) _split_cluster_x single element passthrough and side-by-side split; (c) _crop_regions: full-page (0,0,1,1) crop equals image size, padding clamps at edges, region producing zero-width/height crop; (d) _elements_in_region center-in vs center-out and malformed-bbox skip. Consider hypothesis for the invariant that clusters partition input and region bboxes stay within [0,1].

title: test_auto_start_success leaks module-global _omniparser_process (MagicMock) with no teardown
description: On the success path _ensure_omniparser_server assigns the module global _omniparser_process = proc (vision.py:459). In test_auto_start_success the proc is a MagicMock and atexit.register is patched (line 3839), so the normal atexit cleanup is suppressed and nothing restores the global. There is no fixture or finally clause resetting knowledge_base.vision._omniparser_process after the test (the only reference to it in tests is the patch context manager at line 3901 inside a DIFFERENT test). After this test runs, the module global holds a stale MagicMock for the rest of the session. This is a shared-mutable-state leak that can cause inter-test ordering dependence: a later test invoking _ensure_omniparser_server or _shutdown_omniparser_server could observe stale state. _omniparser_lock is also module-global. Note test_atexit_terminates_server (3895) correctly uses patch() to scope its mutation; test_auto_start_success does not.
suggested_fix: Add an autouse fixture (function-scoped) that snapshots and restores knowledge_base.vision._omniparser_process around each auto-start test, or wrap the global in a patch('knowledge_base.vision._omniparser_process', None) context within test_auto_start_success and assert/reset it after. Best: a reset fixture in the TestOmniParserAutoStart class that yields then sets _omniparser_process back to None.

title: configure_vision invalid-URL-scheme rejection path is untested
description: configure_vision validates base_url scheme and raises ValidationError(f"Invalid URL scheme: {parsed.scheme!r}. Use http or https.") for non-http(s) schemes (vision.py:137-140). The test suite covers the happy roundtrip (test_configure_vision_roundtrip, test_get_vision_config_strips_v1) but has no negative test for this branch: all pytest.raises(ValidationError) matches in test_vision.py are for 'out of range', 'not found', '[Aa]bsolute', and '[Ee]xecutable' (lines 1258-1545) -- none assert the scheme rejection. The error path that protects against e.g. ftp://, file://, or javascript: URLs is unverified.
suggested_fix: Add a parametrized negative test: for scheme in [ftp://host, file:///etc/passwd, javascript:alert(1), '' ] assert configure_vision(conn, base_url=...) raises ValidationError matching 'scheme'. Also add a positive case confirming https:// is accepted and stored.

title: Malformed-metadata JSON-decode fallback paths untested in _collect_extracted_images and _extract_captions
description: Both _collect_extracted_images (vision.py:1278-1281: except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError): continue) and _extract_captions (vision.py:1357-1360: except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError): meta = {}) defensively swallow bad chunk metadata. No test exercises these branches: grep for 'JSONDecodeError|not json|malformed|invalid metadata' in test_vision.py returns nothing, and the test_collect_extracted_images_* triplet (deduplicates / returns_images_grouped_by_page / skips_missing_files) only covers well-formed metadata. A regression that turned these silent-continue paths into a hard error (or stopped tolerating NULL/non-JSON metadata) would go undetected.
suggested_fix: Insert a chunk with metadata='not-json' (and one with metadata=NULL / non-string) for the source_uri, then assert _collect_extracted_images skips it without raising and _extract_captions returns an empty/partial CaptionMap without raising. Pair with a valid chunk to confirm the bad row is skipped, not the whole result.

title: estimate_figures_time docstring claims an {"error": ...} return that the function never produces
description: The docstring states 'Returns {"error": ...} on validation failure.' (line 2100). The function body has no try/except: validation is delegated to _resolve_paper_context (raises NotFoundError) and _validate_pages (raises ValidationError) at lines 2102-2103, and those exceptions propagate uncaught. On the success path the function returns a dict with keys extracted_images/vector_pages/mixed_vector_regions/estimated_seconds/has_omniparser/omniparser_server_healthy (lines 2128-2135) — never an 'error' key. The {"error": ...} dict is actually produced by the route wrapper in src/knowledge_base/routes/extraction.py:245-247, which catches KnowledgeBaseError. The docstring misattributes the wrapper's error-handling contract to this function; a caller relying on it (checking for an 'error' key instead of catching the exception) would crash on validation failure.
suggested_fix: Replace the line 'Returns {"error": ...} on validation failure.' with an accurate Returns/Raises description: the function returns the ETA dict on success and raises NotFoundError / ValidationError on invalid paper_id or page bounds (which the MCP route wrapper converts to an error dict).

title: extract_figures (public API) docstring omits Args, Returns, and Raises
description: extract_figures is one of the four __all__ exports (line 39) and is the module's main entry point. Its docstring (lines 2145-2153) describes the pipeline narratively but documents none of its four call parameters (paper_id, pages, confirmed, on_progress), none of its three distinct return-dict shapes (the ETA-gate dict with confirm_required/estimated_seconds at lines 2184-2188; the early empty-result dict at line 2159; the full result dict at lines 2252-2267), and none of the exceptions it raises (NotFoundError / ValidationError via _resolve_paper_context and _validate_pages at lines 2155-2160). Many internal helpers in the same module (e.g. _render_page:808-820, _crop_regions:651-661, _detect_mixed_page_vector_regions:1160-1177, _collect_extracted_images:1259-1267) use full Google-style Args/Returns sections, so the public API is documented less thoroughly than the private helpers.
suggested_fix: Add Google-style Args (paper_id, pages [0-indexed], confirmed, on_progress), Returns (note the three possible dict shapes: ETA-gate confirmation dict, empty-pages short-circuit dict, and full result summary), and Raises (NotFoundError, ValidationError) sections to match the module's prevailing docstring style.

title: configure_vision (public API) has a one-line docstring missing Args/Returns and the query-on-None behavior
description: configure_vision is a public __all__ export (line 37). Its docstring is the single line 'Configure vision model settings.' (line 127). It does not document its parameters (model, base_url), the ValidationError raised on a non-http(s) URL scheme (lines 137-140), the returned dict shape (_get_vision_config output: keys 'model' and 'base_url'), or the documented behavior that calling with no arguments queries the current configuration (described in docs/usage/figure-extraction.md:55). This contrasts with the sibling public function configure_omniparser (lines 196-208), which has a full Args section. The chosen Google docstring style is applied inconsistently across the two public configure_* functions.
suggested_fix: Expand the docstring with Args (model: new vision model or None to leave unchanged; base_url: OpenAI-compatible endpoint or None), Returns (dict with current 'model' and 'base_url'), Raises (ValidationError on non-http/https base_url scheme), and a note that calling with both args None returns the current configuration.

title: N+1 query in deduplication loop — one SELECT per chunk
description: In _produce_and_insert_chunks, deduplication issues a separate `SELECT id FROM chunks WHERE content_hash = ?` for every produced chunk (line 364-366), inside a per-item Python loop. A large PDF can produce hundreds of chunks, yielding hundreds of sequential SQLite round-trips on the hot ingest path. Because `content_hash` carries a `UNIQUE` constraint (verified in db.py:189: `content_hash TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE`), each hash maps to at most one row, so the whole set can be resolved in a single batched query: collect all `item[2]` hashes, run one `SELECT id, content_hash FROM chunks WHERE content_hash IN ({ph})` via the existing `_batched_select` helper, build a {hash: id} dict, then partition `items` in memory. This collapses N queries into ceil(N/999) and removes per-chunk latency.
suggested_fix: Replace the per-item loop with a single batched lookup: `existing_rows = _batched_select(conn, 'SELECT id, content_hash FROM chunks WHERE content_hash IN ({ph})', [it[2] for it in items])`, build `hash_to_id = {r['content_hash']: r['id'] for r in existing_rows}`, then iterate items once: if `it[2] in hash_to_id`, append id to deferred_session_links (when session_id is not None) and bump skipped; else append to new_items.

title: Quadratic entity re-link loop with per-iteration regex compilation
description: The methods/datasets/metrics re-link in reingest_file is a triple-nested loop: for each table, for each affected entity, for each new chunk it calls `re.search(r'\b' + re.escape(entity['name']) + r'\b', nc['content'])` (lines 616-627). With E affected entities and C new chunks this is O(E*C) regex executions, and the pattern is rebuilt and re-compiled from a string on every single iteration (re's internal cache holds only 512 patterns and is keyed by the exact string, so distinct entity names thrash it). Two compounding costs: (1) the E*C scan, (2) repeated compilation. Precompile one pattern per entity outside the chunk loop: `pat = re.compile(r'\b' + re.escape(entity['name']) + r'\b')` before `for nc in new_chunks`, then `pat.search(nc['content'])`. This removes redundant compilation entirely and is the cheap, safe half of the fix.
suggested_fix: Hoist compilation: inside `for entity in affected:` compute `pattern = re.compile(r'\b' + re.escape(entity['name']) + r'\b')` once, then call `pattern.search(nc['content'])` in the inner loop. For a deeper fix, build the compiled patterns once and break on first match (already done) — the win is avoiding re-compilation per chunk.
references: src/knowledge_base/ingest.py:586-594 (deduplicate=False ensures new_chunks is the freshly inserted set)

title: zip(items, embeddings) silently truncates on length mismatch
description: `for (idx, chunk_text, chunk_hash, meta_json), emb_vec in zip(items, embeddings)` (line 384) pairs produced chunks with embeddings returned by `_embed_with_config`. `zip` without `strict=True` stops at the shorter sequence: if a provider returns fewer embeddings than texts (e.g. partial Ollama/OpenAI batch failure, or a bug in `embed`'s response parsing — see embeddings.py:194 which maps over provider `data`), the trailing chunks are silently dropped, never inserted, and `len(items)` is still reported as `chunks_added` (line 399) — an over-count. The failure is invisible: no exception, no log, chunks just vanish.
