Python 3.12 (project targets 3.12+) supports `strict=True`, which raises ValueError on mismatch, converting silent data loss into a loud, debuggable failure. Same hardening applies wherever embedding count must equal text count.
suggested_fix: Use `zip(items, embeddings, strict=True)` so a length divergence raises ValueError instead of silently dropping chunks. Alternatively assert `len(embeddings) == len(texts_to_embed)` right after the embed call (line 382) with a clear message.
references: src/knowledge_base/embeddings.py:194 (provider response mapped to embedding list)
src/knowledge_base/ingest.py:399 (returns len(items) as chunks_added, assuming all inserted)

title: chunk_index carries pre-dedup positions, leaving gaps after filtering
description: Items are enumerated before deduplication (`items.append((i, ...))` at e.g. lines 321/349/356), so each item's first element is its position in the full pre-dedup list. After dedup removes already-seen chunks (lines 361-373), the surviving items keep their original `i`, and that `i` is passed as `chunk_index` into `_insert_chunk` (line 391). The result is a non-contiguous `chunk_index` sequence for a partially-deduped document (e.g. 0,2,5,...). reingest_file relies on `ORDER BY chunk_index` for both abstract re-linking (line 599) and entity re-linking (line 612), and ingest path dedup means the FIRST chunk (index 0) may itself be deduped away — so `ORDER BY chunk_index LIMIT 1` would not necessarily return the document's logical first chunk after a future reingest. Ordering is preserved (still monotonic), so this is low severity, but the gaps are a latent surprise for any consumer that treats chunk_index as a dense 0..N-1 range.
suggested_fix: If chunk_index is meant to be a dense per-document ordinal, assign it after dedup using `enumerate(items)` at insert time rather than carrying the pre-dedup `idx`. If gaps are intentional (stable identity across reingests), document that contract near the enumerate sites.
references: src/knowledge_base/ingest.py:611-614 (ORDER BY chunk_index for entity relink)

title: _produce_and_insert_chunks is a ~130 LOC multi-responsibility function
description: Current state: a single ~130-line function performs text extraction (PDF/markdown dispatch), image-dir teardown, chunk production across three strategies (AST / semantic / mechanical), metadata assembly, content-hash deduplication, embedding, and row insertion. It has four exit points and three independent dispatch ladders on source_type. Target state: decompose into a small pipeline — _extract_text(source_type, path) -> (text, page_map, image_dir), _produce_chunk_items(source_type, text, page_map, image_dir, path) -> list[ChunkItem], _dedup_items(conn, items, ...) -> (new_items, skipped, deferred), and _embed_and_insert(conn, items, ...). The top function then reads as four named steps. Pattern name: Extract Method / Compose Method (pipeline). Motivation: the function violates SRP, exceeds the 50-LOC smell threshold by ~2.6x, and the source_type dispatch is duplicated (lines 291, 295, 307, 322) so behavior for a given type is scattered. Estimated scope: one module, ~4 new private helpers, no public API change, tests in tests/test_ingest.py should still pass unchanged. Prerequisites: introduce a ChunkItem type (see ingest/design-chunk-tuple-obsession) to make helper signatures clean. Risk: medium — the dedup/embed/insert ordering and the early-return-on-empty-chunks semantics (lines 335-336, 354-355, 377-378) must be preserved exactly; embedding cost and transaction (no-commit) boundary must not shift.
suggested_fix: Split into _extract_text, _produce_chunk_items, _dedup_items, _embed_and_insert helpers; keep _produce_and_insert_chunks as the 4-step orchestrator preserving exit-point semantics.

title: reingest_file is a ~150 LOC orchestrator mixing FK-cleanup, session preservation, re-link
description: Current state: reingest_file spans ~150 lines and inlines six numbered FK-cleanup blocks (papers, relationships, conclusions, methods/datasets/metrics, entity_mentions), historical-session capture, cascade delete, re-ingest, paper re-link, entity re-link by regex, and paper_paths hash update. Target state: extract _cleanup_fk_refs(conn, old_ids) -> ReingestCleanupState (capturing affected_paper_ids and affected_entities), _relink_papers(conn, source_uri, affected_paper_ids), and _relink_entities(conn, source_uri, affected_entities); reingest_file then orchestrates capture -> delete -> reingest -> relink. Pattern name: Extract Method + introduce a small state object (Parameter Object) for the captured cleanup state. Motivation: the function carries cross-cutting state (affected_paper_ids, affected_entities, historical_sessions) across ~150 lines, making the delete/reingest/relink invariant hard to verify; the numbered-comment structure is a code smell signalling latent sub-functions. Estimated scope: one module, ~3 helpers, public signature unchanged. Prerequisites: none, though a ReingestCleanupState dataclass clarifies the contract. Risk: medium — ordering is load-bearing: captures must happen before delete_chunks_cascade (line 576), re-link must happen after re-ingest; the chunk_strategy / session-union behavior must be preserved.
suggested_fix: Extract _cleanup_fk_refs, _relink_papers, _relink_entities; pass captured state via a small dataclass; keep delete->reingest->relink ordering intact.

title: Chunk items modeled as positional tuple[int,str,str,str] (primitive obsession)
description: Current state: chunk items are passed around as anonymous 4-tuples (chunk_index, text, content_hash, metadata_json) built at lines 311/321, 343-351, and 356, then later destructured positionally at line 384 (`for (idx, chunk_text, chunk_hash, meta_json), emb_vec in zip(...)`) and indexed by magic position at line 364 (`item[2]` = content_hash) and line 381 (`item[1]` = text). Target state: a frozen dataclass `@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) class ChunkItem: index: int; text: str; content_hash: str; metadata: str`. Pattern name: Replace Tuple/Data Clump with Data Object. Motivation: positional indexing (`item[1]`, `item[2]`) is opaque and fragile — reordering or extending the tuple silently breaks the dedup lookup; the type annotation `tuple[int, str, str, str]` (lines 311, 361) does not document field meaning. Estimated scope: one module, replace tuple construction/destructuring sites; metadata stays a JSON string field (could later become a dict). Prerequisites: none. Risk: low-medium — purely structural; the embedded JSON-string-as-field is still primitive obsession but out of scope for this change.
suggested_fix: Introduce a frozen ChunkItem dataclass with named fields (index, text, content_hash, metadata) and replace tuple positions item[1]/item[2] and the zip destructuring with attribute access.

title: source_type and chunk_strategy are stringly-typed (enum candidates)
description: Current state: source_type values ('pdf','markdown','code') are bare strings returned by _detect_source_type (lines 63-80) and compared throughout (lines 291, 295, 307, 322); chunk_strategy ('semantic','mechanical') likewise (lines 193, 290-292, 323). Target state: enum.StrEnum SourceType and ChunkStrategy (StrEnum keeps DB/string interop while gaining type-checking and discoverability). Pattern name: Replace Magic String with Enum. Motivation: typos in these strings are silent (no checker catches `source_type == 'PDF'`); StrEnum lets pyright flag invalid comparisons and centralizes the vocabulary that is currently duplicated across this module and the chunking/db layers. Estimated scope: cross-module — these strings persist to the DB (chunks.source_type, chunks.chunk_strategy) and are read by search/papers, so a StrEnum must serialize identically; a focused first pass can confine the enum to ingest.py with `.value` at DB boundaries. Prerequisites: confirm DB-stored values and any consumers (search.py, papers.py) tolerate StrEnum equality (StrEnum == str holds). Risk: medium — DB round-trip must yield identical literal strings; partial adoption risks mixing str and enum at comparison sites.
suggested_fix: Define enum.StrEnum SourceType {PDF, MARKDOWN, CODE} and ChunkStrategy {SEMANTIC, MECHANICAL}; use them in _detect_source_type and comparisons, ensuring StrEnum values match the persisted literals.

title: Magic numbers in pdf_image_dir hashing/truncation lack named constants
description:
Current state: pdf_image_dir uses bare literals 8192 (read block size, line 210), [:16] (hash prefix length, line 212), and [:64] (sanitized stem cap, line 213); _extract_pdf_markdown uses `+ 2` for the '

' separator offset (line 258, with an inline comment). Target state: module-level named constants, e.g. _HASH_READ_BLOCK = 8192, _DIR_HASH_PREFIX_LEN = 16, _DIR_STEM_MAX_LEN = 64, and _PAGE_SEPARATOR = '

' with offset derived as len(_PAGE_SEPARATOR). Pattern name: Replace Magic Number with Named Constant. Motivation: the 16-char hash prefix and 64-char stem cap define an on-disk directory naming contract for extracted figures; encoding them as named constants documents the contract and prevents drift between the directory writer and any reader. Light, mechanical, low risk. Estimated scope: a handful of literal replacements within this module. Prerequisites: none. Risk: low — values unchanged; the `+ 2` should be tied to the actual separator string used at line 259's join to avoid a hidden coupling.
suggested_fix: Hoist 8192, 16, 64 to named module constants and derive the page-separator offset from a single '\n\n' constant shared with the join at line 259.

title: Entity re-link is a triple-nested loop recompiling regex per chunk
description: Current state: re-linking methods/datasets/metrics iterates tables x affected-entities x new-chunks (lines 616-618) and calls re.search with a freshly built pattern `r'\b' + re.escape(entity['name']) + r'\b'` inside the innermost loop (lines 619-622), recompiling the same per-entity pattern once per chunk. Complexity is O(tables * entities * chunks * content_len). Target state: precompile one re.Pattern per entity name (re.compile(...)) outside the chunk loop and reuse it; optionally hoist new_chunks content into a list once. Pattern name: Hoist Invariant / Precompute. Motivation: regex (re)compilation in the hot inner loop is wasted work; for large re-ingests with many entities and chunks this is the dominant cost of reingest_file's tail. This is a structural/maintainability concern (clear separation of 'build matcher' from 'scan chunks') as much as a micro-perf one. Estimated scope: local to this block; wrap entity-name compilation above the inner loop. Prerequisites: none. Risk: low — semantics (first-match-wins via break) preserved if the precompiled pattern is identical.
suggested_fix: Precompile the word-boundary pattern per entity once before the chunk loop and reuse the compiled object inside; keep the first-match break.

title: _update_folder_summary_safe references module logger defined 10 lines later
description: Current state: _update_folder_summary_safe (lines 38-45) calls module-level `logger` (line 43) but `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` is not defined until line 48, after the function. This works only because the name is resolved at call time (late binding), not at def time, but the ordering is surprising and inconsistent with the convention of declaring module loggers near the top of the file. Target state: move `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` to immediately after the imports (above line 38), as is conventional. Pattern name: Reorder Declarations (readability). Motivation: a reader scanning top-down sees a logger use before its definition; placing the logger before all functions matches PEP-8/common practice and avoids the implicit late-binding dependency. Estimated scope: move one line. Prerequisites: none. Risk: none — pure reordering, behavior identical.
suggested_fix: Move the `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` assignment from line 48 to just below the import block (before _update_folder_summary_safe).

title: _detect_source_type silently defaults unknown extensions to 'markdown'
description: Current state: _detect_source_type returns 'markdown' for any extension not in the pdf/markdown/code sets (fallthrough at line 80), so e.g. a .docx, .csv, or binary file is treated as UTF-8 markdown and read via _extract_markdown_text with errors='replace' (line 266). This is a silent misclassification rather than an explicit decision. Target state: either make the fallback explicit and logged (logger.info on fallback), or restrict ingest to known extensions and raise/skip unknowns; the directory walker already filters by extension (lines 657-664) but ingest_file can be called directly with arbitrary paths. Pattern name: Make Implicit Decision Explicit (guard clause / fail-fast). Motivation: 'unknown -> markdown' hides ingestion of unsupported file types as garbled text, which then pollutes the index; the choice should be a logged policy, not an unannounced default. Estimated scope: one function plus a log line or a raised error and a caller decision. Prerequisites: decide the desired contract (skip vs. best-effort markdown) — this is a behavior change, so confirm intent. Risk: medium — changing the fallback to raise would alter ingest_file behavior for callers relying on best-effort text extraction.
suggested_fix: Make the unknown-extension path explicit: log the fallback at INFO (or skip/raise per chosen policy) instead of silently returning 'markdown'.

title: _detect_source_type extension mapping is untested
description: _detect_source_type maps file extensions to source_type ('pdf' | 'markdown' | 'code') across three branches plus a fallback. No test in tests/test_ingest.py calls it directly or asserts its full mapping. The '.py' -> 'code' path is exercised indirectly by test_ingest_python_file_uses_ast, but the rest of the map is unverified: '.txt'/'.typ'/'.rst' -> 'markdown'; the entire code-extension set ('.rs', '.cpp', '.c', '.h', '.hpp', '.toml', '.yaml', '.yml', '.json') -> 'code'; '.pdf' -> 'pdf'; and the unknown-extension default -> 'markdown' (line 80). This is pure deterministic branching logic with no I/O — the highest-value, lowest-cost candidate for parametrization. A regression that, say, drops '.json' from the code list or changes the default would pass the current suite.
suggested_fix: Add a parametrized unit test: @pytest.mark.parametrize('name,expected', [('a.pdf','pdf'), ('a.md','markdown'), ('a.txt','markdown'), ('a.typ','markdown'), ('a.rst','markdown'), ('a.py','code'), ('a.rs','code'), ('a.cpp','code'), ('a.c','code'), ('a.h','code'), ('a.hpp','code'), ('a.toml','code'), ('a.yaml','code'), ('a.yml','code'), ('a.json','code'), ('a.PDF','pdf'), ('a.unknown','markdown'), ('a','markdown')]) asserting _detect_source_type(Path(name)) == expected. Include an uppercase-extension case to lock in the suffix.lower() behavior.

title: _embed_with_config matryoshka base_dim branch never exercised
description: _embed_with_config has two branches gated on get_active_space(conn) returning a space with a 'matryoshka_base_dim' (lines 92-102). When base_dim is set, it embeds at base_dim then truncates every vector to cfg['dim'] via truncate_embedding, mapping None passthrough for zero-norm vectors. No test in tests/test_ingest.py creates an active space with matryoshka_base_dim, so this branch and the truncate-on-each-vector logic are completely uncovered in the ingest pipeline. truncate_embedding is unit-tested in isolation (tests/test_embeddings.py:280) but never integrated here. The only test that references _embed_with_config (test_embed_failure_does_not_leave_orphan_session_rows) patches it with a RuntimeError side_effect, so the real function body is never run with a configured space. A bug in the base_dim/truncate path (e.g. truncating to the wrong dim, or mishandling the None entry inside the list comprehension at line 101) would ship silently.
suggested_fix: Add a test that sets an active embedding space with matryoshka_base_dim > cfg['dim'], patches knowledge_base.ingest.embed to return base_dim-length vectors (and one None), then calls _embed_with_config directly and asserts: (a) embed() was invoked with expected_dim == base_dim, (b) returned non-None vectors have len == cfg['dim'], (c) the None entry is preserved as None. Add a companion test for the non-matryoshka branch asserting expected_dim == cfg['dim'] and no truncation.

title: _extract_pdf_markdown RuntimeError/OSError fallback untested
description: _extract_pdf_markdown has two fallback paths to _extract_pdf_text: ImportError (lines 236-238, covered by test_extract_pdf_markdown_fallback_on_import_error) and (RuntimeError, OSError) raised by pymupdf4llm.to_markdown (lines 260-262). The latter is not covered — no test makes a mocked to_markdown raise RuntimeError or OSError. This is a distinct error path with its own logging and fallback, and the second-most-likely real-world failure (a corrupt/locked PDF causing pymupdf4llm to throw at runtime rather than import time).
suggested_fix: Add a test that injects a mock pymupdf4llm whose to_markdown.side_effect = RuntimeError('mupdf error') (and a parametrized variant with OSError), patches _extract_pdf_text to a sentinel, and asserts _extract_pdf_markdown returns (sentinel_text, {}) and that the warning is logged. Reuse the _make_mock_pymupdf4llm helper, overriding to_markdown.side_effect.

title: Semantic PDF chunking branch (_chunk_by_section) never exercised end-to-end
description: In _produce_and_insert_chunks, effective_strategy becomes 'semantic' only when source_type == 'pdf' AND config chunk_strategy == 'semantic' (lines 290-292), which then dispatches to _chunk_by_section (lines 323-328) instead of _chunk_markdown. The existing strategy tests cover only: mechanical default (test_ingest_file_mechanical_default), and semantic config with a NON-pdf file (test_ingest_file_semantic_nonpdf) which by design stays mechanical. The actual semantic-PDF path — the one combination where _chunk_by_section runs — is never tested. So the section-based chunker is never reached through the ingest pipeline, and the chunk_strategy='semantic' value written to the chunks row for PDFs is unverified.
suggested_fix: Add a test that sets config chunk_strategy='semantic', mocks pymupdf4llm (via _make_mock_pymupdf4llm with _SIMPLE_PAGES), ingests a .pdf, and asserts: (a) chunks were produced, (b) chunks.chunk_strategy == 'semantic', and ideally (c) _chunk_by_section was invoked (patch knowledge_base.ingest._chunk_by_section and assert called, knowledge_base.ingest._chunk_markdown not called).

title: ingest_directory extension filter, custom extensions, and nesting untested
description: ingest_directory is tested only via test_ingest_directory_shares_session_id and test_ingest_directory_explicit_session_id, both using a flat directory of .md files with default extensions. Three behaviors are uncovered: (1) the default extension filter SKIPPING non-matching files (e.g. a .py or .docx file present in the directory must be ignored — line 664 condition f.suffix.lower() in extensions); (2) the custom extensions argument (passing extensions={'.py'} or similar) — the entire 'extensions is None' default-assignment branch vs caller-supplied branch (lines 657-658); (3) recursive rglob traversal into nested subdirectories and the affected_folders per-folder summary batching (lines 663-673). A regression that flattens rglob to glob, or that ignores the caller's extensions, would pass the current suite.
suggested_fix: Add tests: (a) directory with mixed files (.md + .py + .json + a no-extension file) ingested with default extensions; assert only .md/.txt/.typ/.rst/.pdf-matching files produced chunks and the others were skipped. (b) same directory ingested with extensions={'.py'}; assert only the .py file was ingested. (c) nested layout (dir/sub1/a.md, dir/sub2/b.md); assert both ingested and that affected_folders / folder-summary update covers both parent dirs (patch _update_folder_summary_safe and assert called once per distinct folder).

title: PDF image-dir pre-clean (rmtree) branch on re-ingest is untested
description: When ingesting a PDF, _produce_and_insert_chunks computes pdf_image_dir(path) and, if it already exists, calls shutil.rmtree(image_dir) before re-extracting (lines 296-298). No test exercises the image_dir.exists() == True branch — the PDF tests use mocked pymupdf4llm whose to_markdown does not create the image dir, and test_reingest_pdf_uses_markdown does not assert anything about image-dir cleanup. The stale-figure cleanup-on-reingest path is therefore unverified; a regression that removes or inverts this rmtree (leaving stale extracted figures) would go undetected.
suggested_fix: Add a test that pre-creates pdf_image_dir(pdf) with a sentinel file, then ingests/re-ingests the PDF (with mocked pymupdf4llm). Assert the sentinel file no longer exists after ingest (the dir was rmtree'd and recreated). Patch shutil.rmtree to a spy if you prefer to assert the call directly while keeping the mocked extractor.

title: Empty-chunk early-returns for PDF and fixed-size text are partially untested
description: _produce_and_insert_chunks has two early-return-(0,0,[]) guards when a chunker yields nothing: the PDF/markdown branch (line 335-336, 'if not md_chunks') and the fixed-size branch (line 354-355, 'if not fixed_chunks'). The fixed-size empty case is indirectly reachable (chunk_text returns [] for whitespace, tested at unit level in test_chunk_text_empty), but neither early-return is asserted through ingest_file (e.g. ingesting an empty or whitespace-only .md / a PDF whose mocked extractor returns pages that chunk to nothing). The result-shape on a zero-chunk ingest (chunks_added==0, chunks_skipped==0, and that no duplicate_of_paper_id lookup misfires) is unverified.
suggested_fix: Add a test ingesting a whitespace-only markdown file and assert result == {'file': ..., 'chunks_added': 0, 'chunks_skipped': 0} with no exception and no chunks/vec rows. Add a PDF variant where the mocked pymupdf4llm returns pages whose text chunks to an empty list and assert the (0,0,[]) early return is hit (no chunks inserted).

title: duplicate_of_paper_id OSError-on-hash and no-matching-paper branches untested
description: The duplicate-detection block in ingest_file (lines 432-446) runs only when added==0 and skipped>0. test_ingest_detects_duplicate_by_hash covers the happy path (existing paper found -> duplicate_of_paper_id set). Two sub-branches are uncovered: (1) compute_file_hash raises OSError -> file_hash=None -> result omits duplicate_of_paper_id (lines 436-438); (2) file_hash computed but no paper_paths row matches -> existing_paper is None -> key not added (lines 440-445). Both are negative paths controlling whether the optional result key appears, and a caller may branch on its presence.
suggested_fix: Add a test where content is fully deduped but no paper was registered for that hash; assert 'duplicate_of_paper_id' not in result. Add a second test patching knowledge_base.papers.compute_file_hash to raise OSError and assert the dedup result still returns cleanly without the key.

title: Repeated DB setup boilerplate; no shared conn fixture (no conftest.py)
description: There is no tests/conftest.py, and the three-line db setup block (db_path = tmp_path/'test.db'; conn = get_connection(db_path); init_schema(conn)) is duplicated in well over 100 tests in this file. The @patch('knowledge_base.folder_summaries.embed', _fake_embed) + @patch('knowledge_base.ingest.embed', _fake_embed) decorator pair is likewise repeated dozens of times. This is fixture-quality debt: no factory for an initialized connection, no autouse fixture for the embed mock, and the connection is never explicitly closed (relying on tmp_path teardown + WAL). It does not cause incorrect results today, but it inflates maintenance cost and makes it easy to forget the embed mock (a forgotten mock would attempt a real Ollama call — a hidden network dependency).
suggested_fix: Introduce a function-scoped fixture in a new tests/conftest.py: `@pytest.fixture\ndef conn(tmp_path): c = get_connection(tmp_path/'test.db'); init_schema(c); yield c; c.close()` with an annotated return type (Iterator[sqlite3.Connection]). Add an autouse fixture that patches both embed symbols to _fake_embed (move _fake_embed there too) so individual tests cannot accidentally hit the network. Migrate incrementally.

title: Public ingest_file() has no docstring
description: ingest_file is part of the module public API (listed in __all__ at line 53 and imported by routes/ingestion.py:12 and the CLI) yet has no docstring at all. The function has a non-trivial contract: it returns a dict whose keys vary ('file', 'chunks_added', 'chunks_skipped', and conditionally 'duplicate_of_paper_id' when added==0 and skipped>0, set at line 445), commits the transaction, and accepts a private-looking _skip_folder_summary flag (line 407) whose purpose (suppressing per-file folder-summary recomputation during directory batches) is non-obvious. None of this is documented. The return type annotation is the bare 'dict' (line 408), so callers have no signal about the dict's shape either.
suggested_fix: Add a docstring describing the parameters (notably source_type auto-detection when None, and the _skip_folder_summary flag), the returned dict's keys including the conditional 'duplicate_of_paper_id', and the side effect that the transaction is committed before returning. Match the file's existing freeform-prose docstring style (e.g. a 'Returns ...' sentence as used in _produce_and_insert_chunks).

title: Public ingest_directory() has no docstring
description: ingest_directory is part of the module public API (listed in __all__ at line 53, imported by routes/ingestion.py:12) but has no docstring. Undocumented behaviors a caller cannot infer from the signature: the default extension set defaults to {'.pdf', '.md', '.txt', '.typ', '.rst'} (line 658) when extensions is None; a fresh session_id (uuid4) is auto-generated when session_id is None (lines 659-660), so all ingested files in the call share one session; folder summaries are batched and recomputed once per affected folder at the end (lines 671-673) rather than per file. The return type 'list[dict]' (line 654) is documented only by annotation, with no description of element shape.
suggested_fix: Add a docstring covering: recursive walk via rglob filtered by extensions; the default extension set; the auto-generated shared session_id when session_id is None; the batched folder-summary update; and the returned list of per-file result dicts (same shape as ingest_file's return).

title: reingest_file() docstring omits NotFoundError it raises
description: reingest_file raises NotFoundError at line 513 when no chunks exist for the source_uri. This is a tested public contract (tests/test_ingest.py:724 asserts `pytest.raises(NotFoundError, match="No chunks found")`). The function's docstring (lines 500-504) describes the delete-and-re-ingest behavior and FK cleanup but never mentions that it raises NotFoundError for a not-previously-ingested path, leaving the failure mode undocumented for callers.
suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the docstring documenting that NotFoundError is raised when no chunks exist for the file's source_uri (i.e., the file was never ingested). Keep the file's freeform-prose style rather than introducing a formal 'Raises:' section unless adopting Google-style sections module-wide (see related style finding).

title: _extract_markdown_text() has no docstring
description: _extract_markdown_text is the only private helper in the module lacking a docstring; every other private helper (_detect_source_type, _embed_with_config, _extract_pdf_text, _extract_pdf_markdown, _insert_chunk, etc.) carries a one-line description. The omission is a minor internal-consistency gap. Its behavior (read file as UTF-8 with errors='replace') is non-trivial enough that the error-handling policy is worth a one-liner, especially as the sibling _extract_pdf_text at line 196 is documented.
suggested_fix: Add a one-line docstring, e.g. "Read a markdown/text file as UTF-8, replacing undecodable bytes." to match the surrounding helpers.

title: Module uses freeform-prose docstrings, no structured sections
description: ingest.py contains zero structured docstring sections (no Args:, Returns:, or Raises: headers anywhere in the file; verified by grep). Return values are documented via inline 'Returns ...' prose sentences (e.g. lines 139, 287-288) and parameters are not separately enumerated. Sibling modules in the same package use Google-style sections (Args:/Returns:/Raises: present in vision.py, papers.py, search.py, web.py, keywords.py), so this module diverges from the codebase's emerging docstring convention. The freeform style is internally consistent within this file, so this is a style/consistency observation rather than a correctness defect.
suggested_fix: Decide on one docstring convention package-wide. If standardizing on Google-style (as several modules already do), migrate ingest.py's public functions to Args:/Returns:/Raises: sections; otherwise leave the consistent freeform prose as-is. No action required if freeform prose is the accepted norm for this module.

title: Quadratic duplicate handling in _collect_entity_mentions
description: When an entity key is already in `seen`, the else-branch (lines 495-503) does a linear scan over the entire `mentions` list to find the matching entry, then for each incoming surface_form does an `if sf not in m["surface_forms"]` membership test (another linear scan). With N distinct entities each appearing in multiple chunks, this is O(N^2 * K) on the number of entity mentions. For a long paper map-reduced into many chunks, the merge step degrades super-linearly. The `seen` set is already maintained for O(1) existence checks, but the actual mention object is re-found via scan rather than looked up.
suggested_fix: Maintain a `key -> mention` dict (e.g. `by_key`) alongside `seen` so the merge target is fetched in O(1): `m = by_key[key]`. Track surface_forms membership with a per-mention set (or a side dict `key -> set`) instead of `sf not in m["surface_forms"]` list scan, appending to the list only for new forms to preserve order. This collapses the merge to O(total mentions).

title: N+1 query pattern in get_entities
description: `get_entities` issues one query to fetch all entities, then loops over them and issues a separate `SELECT ... FROM entity_mentions WHERE entity_id = ?` per entity (lines 1097-1100). For a paper with E resolved entities this is E+1 round-trips to SQLite. A paper with many methods/datasets multiplies the per-query overhead linearly.
suggested_fix: Fetch all mentions in one pass with a single JOIN (`entities LEFT JOIN entity_mentions ON entity_mentions.entity_id = entities.id WHERE entities.paper_id = ?`) and group rows in Python by entity id, or batch the mentions query with `entity_id IN (...)` (respecting the 999-variable batching helper `db._batched_execute` used elsewhere) and bucket results into a dict before assembling the response.

title: N+1 query pattern in compare_papers per shared dataset
description: After finding shared dataset names in one query, `compare_papers` loops over each shared dataset and runs a separate metrics JOIN query per dataset (lines 372-383). For D shared datasets this is D additional round-trips. Additionally `ds_placeholders` (line 372) recomputes the exact same placeholder string already bound to `placeholders` at line 355 on every iteration.
suggested_fix: Run a single metrics query for all shared datasets at once: `WHERE d.name IN (<shared-ds placeholders>) AND m.paper_id IN (<paper placeholders>) ORDER BY d.name, m.name, m.value DESC`, then group rows by dataset name in Python. Hoist the paper-id placeholder string out of the loop (reuse `placeholders`).

title: UPSERT followed by separate SELECT round-trip in _record_entity
description: `_record_entity` performs an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, then issues a second SELECT to retrieve the row id (lines 204-206). It is called once per method and once per dataset inside the per-entity loops of `_store_resolved` (lines 654-676) and `_extract_single_pass` (lines 781-835), doubling the statement count on the write path. The same INSERT-OR-IGNORE-then-fallback-SELECT shape recurs at lines 617-626, 793-805, and 822-835. SQLite supports `RETURNING` (since 3.35; CPython 3.12 bundles a newer SQLite), which collapses each pair into one statement.
suggested_fix: Use `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(...) DO UPDATE SET ... RETURNING id` and read `.fetchone()` from the same cursor, eliminating the follow-up SELECT. For the `INSERT OR IGNORE` sites, `RETURNING id` returns the id on insert; keep a single fallback SELECT only for the conflict-skipped case. Confirm the minimum bundled SQLite version across target deployment platforms before adopting RETURNING unconditionally.

title: Repeated entity_data dict key lookup in hot loop
description: Inside the per-item entity-collection loop, `entity_data[(canonical, etype)]` is recomputed up to 5 times per item (lines 598, 600, 603, 607) — each indexing the defaultdict with a freshly built tuple key and triggering hash/lookup. For documents with many entity mentions this is avoidable repeated work in the inner loop.
suggested_fix: Bind the entry once: `entry = entity_data[(canonical, etype)]` (the defaultdict factory still runs on first access), then mutate `entry["type"]`, `entry["description"]`, `entry["mentions"]` etc. via the local reference.

title: Dynamic SQL table-name interpolation gated only by allowlist (currently safe)
description: _record_entity() builds INSERT and SELECT statements by interpolating the `table` argument directly into the SQL string via f-strings (lines 196 and 205), because SQLite identifiers cannot be parameterized with `?`. This is NOT currently exploitable: the function rejects any value not in the `_ENTITY_TABLES = frozenset({'methods','datasets'})` allowlist at line 193 before the f-string is constructed, and the only callers (record_method, record_dataset) pass hardcoded literals. All actual data values (name, paper_id, description, chunk_id, source) are bound with `?` placeholders. I am reporting this at info severity solely as a defense-in-depth observation: the allowlist is the single point of trust, so any future caller that bypasses or widens it would reintroduce CWE-89. I verified there is no other injectable SQL in the file -- compare_papers (lines 355-383) interpolates only computed `?,?,...` placeholder strings derived from len(paper_ids), never values, and every other query is fully parameterized.
suggested_fix: No code change required; the allowlist guard is correct. To harden as defense-in-depth, optionally replace the free-form `table: str` parameter with a typed Enum / Literal['methods','datasets'] so the type system (not a runtime check) enforces the constraint, making it impossible for a future caller to pass an arbitrary string. Keep the runtime allowlist check regardless.

title: __all__ re-exports underscore-prefixed internal validators
description: Current state: `__all__` lists `_validate_extraction` and `_validate_resolution`. The leading underscore declares them private-by-convention, yet `__all__` membership re-exports them as part of the module's public `import *` surface — a contradictory signal. The brief asks specifically whether internal funcs are `_`-prefixed and whether `__all__` is minimal; these two entries violate both. Verified the only callers are tests (tests/test_extraction.py), which import them by explicit name (`from knowledge_base.extraction import _validate_extraction`) — that mechanism is independent of `__all__`, so removing them from `__all__` does not break the tests. Target state: drop both from `__all__`; keep the underscore names. Pattern: minimal public API surface. Motivation: a `_name` in `__all__` confuses linters, IDEs, and downstream consumers about the contract. Risk: none (no `import *` consumer relies on them).
suggested_fix: Remove the `"_validate_extraction"` and `"_validate_resolution"` entries from `__all__`. Tests already import them by explicit name, so behavior is unchanged.

title: _store_resolved is a ~180-LOC multi-responsibility function
description: Current state: `_store_resolved` (lines 558-738, ~180 LOC inside one try/except) performs at least five distinct jobs: (1) clear prior extraction, (2) build surface->canonical lookup from resolution groups (568-579), (3) aggregate per-canonical entity_data via a defaultdict-of-dict (581-612), (4) insert into entities + entity_mentions (614-638), (5) write methods/datasets tables with a member-alias fan-out (640-690), (6) write metrics with canonical re-resolution (692-727). Abstraction levels are mixed (raw SQL strings interleaved with dict bookkeeping). Target state: extract `_build_canonical_lookup(resolution) -> dict[tuple[str,str], str]`, `_aggregate_entities(map_results, lookup) -> dict[...]`, `_insert_entities_and_mentions(conn, entity_data, paper_id) -> dict`, `_write_metrics(conn, map_results, lookup, method_map, dataset_map, paper_id) -> int`, leaving `_store_resolved` as a ~25-LOC orchestrator wrapped in the existing transaction. Pattern: Extract Method / Compose Method. Motivation: each helper becomes unit-testable in isolation; the transaction boundary stays in one place. Estimated scope: single module, ~5 new private helpers, plus test additions. Prerequisites: none. Risk: medium — control flow and the metric attribution fan-out (685-690) must be preserved exactly; cover with characterization tests on entity_id_map / method_map ordering before refactoring.
suggested_fix: Decompose into the named private helpers above; keep `conn.commit()/rollback()` boundary in the orchestrator. Add characterization tests asserting current methods_added/datasets_added/metrics_added/entities_resolved counts before extracting.
references: Fowler, Refactoring: Extract Method, Compose Method

title: Single-pass path re-implements the entity/mention/metric write logic
description: Current state: `_extract_single_pass` (755-871, ~116 LOC) inlines its own entities + entity_mentions inserts for methods (778-805) and datasets (807-835) and its own metric writes (837-860). This duplicates the persistence logic already implemented (and resolution-aware) in `_store_resolved` (558-738). The two paths can drift: e.g. single-pass writes entities with `name` as canonical and only the first chunk_id, while the map-reduce path resolves canonicals and records all mention chunks — divergent provenance behavior for the same database. Target state: have the single-pass path build a one-element `map_results` (a single `_validate_extraction` dict tagged with `first_chunk_id`) and an empty resolution `{"groups": []}`, then delegate to `_store_resolved`. `_store_resolved` already handles the no-resolution case (surface_to_canonical empty -> name is its own canonical). Pattern: Substitute Algorithm / Pull Up duplicated logic into one writer. Motivation: single source of truth for persistence; eliminates ~90 LOC and the drift risk; the per-method and per-dataset insert blocks (778-805 vs 807-835) are themselves near-identical and vanish. Estimated scope: rewrite `_extract_single_pass` body to ~20 LOC; verify chunk_id tagging semantics. Prerequisites: ideally do the `_store_resolved` decomposition first so the delegation target is clean. Risk: medium-high — single-pass currently attaches `first_chunk_id` to every entity/metric; the delegated path must reproduce that, and `_store_resolved` prefers description_chunk_id (652) which must remain correct when all chunk_ids equal first_chunk_id.
suggested_fix: Replace the inline persistence in `_extract_single_pass` with: tag `extracted` items' chunk_id = first_chunk_id, wrap as `[extracted]`, call `_store_resolved(conn, paper_id, [extracted], {"groups": []})`, then add `paper_id`. Keep a regression test comparing single-pass output rows against the prior implementation.

title: _extract_map_reduce mixes orchestration with logging/ETA bookkeeping
description: Current state: `_extract_map_reduce` (874-1024, ~150 LOC) interleaves three phases (map submit+collect, resolve, store) with heavy inline logging and ETA arithmetic (932-974). The map phase's progress/ETA logging (~40 LOC) is orthogonal to the orchestration logic. Target state: extract `_run_map_phase(conn-free args...) -> tuple[list[dict], list[dict], float]` returning (ordered_results, errors, total_elapsed) and an internal progress/ETA reporter, leaving `_extract_map_reduce` as a 3-step orchestrator. Pattern: Extract Method, separate cross-cutting logging concern. Motivation: the map phase is the only concurrent section and the only one worth testing in isolation; ETA math (967-974) is hard to see under the orchestration. Estimated scope: 1-2 extracted helpers in-module. Prerequisites: none. Risk: medium — preserve the deterministic `map_results.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])` ordering and the on_progress callback cadence.
suggested_fix: Extract the `with httpx.Client(...)/ThreadPoolExecutor(...)` block (906-980) into `_run_map_phase(chunks, cfg, effective_workers, on_progress)` returning `(ordered_results, errors, total_elapsed)`; keep resolve/store in the orchestrator.

title: _collect_entity_mentions does an O(n^2) linear rescan on duplicate keys
description: Current state: `_collect_entity_mentions` maintains a `seen` set for dedup, but on the duplicate branch (495-503) it linearly scans the entire `mentions` list to find the entry it already knows by `key`, to append new surface_forms. With many repeated entities across chunks this is O(n^2). Target state: keep an auxiliary `mentions_by_key: dict[tuple[str, str], dict]` so both the first-insert and the surface_form-merge are O(1). Pattern: introduce index map / replace linear search with hash lookup. Motivation: removes the only super-linear hotspot in the map-reduce path; also simplifies the branch. Estimated scope: ~8 lines within one function. Prerequisites: none. Risk: low — output list order and content are preserved if the dict is populated alongside the list append.
suggested_fix: Add `mentions_by_key = {}`. On first sight store `mentions_by_key[key] = entry` after appending; on the else branch, fetch `m = mentions_by_key[key]` instead of the `for m in mentions` scan, then merge surface_forms.
references: Effective Python: prefer dict lookups over linear scans

title: Function-local import of papers.get_paper_chunks signals deferred-cycle coupling
description: Current state: `_get_paper_chunks` imports `from .papers import get_paper_chunks` inside the function body rather than at module scope. I verified papers.py does NOT currently import extraction at module level (papers.py imports db, exceptions, utils, and a deferred bibtex import at line 382), so this is not breaking a live cycle today. It is nonetheless a lazy-import pattern that (a) hides a real `extraction -> papers` dependency from the module header, and (b) is the classic guard kept in place to pre-empt a future circular import. It also adds per-call import overhead on a hot path (called from `estimate_extraction_time` and `extract_structure`). Target state: if no cycle exists, hoist to a module-level `from .papers import get_paper_chunks`; if a latent cycle is the reason, document it with a comment so the intent is explicit. Pattern: make dependencies explicit at module boundary. Motivation: module-level imports are the readable contract; silent function-local imports obscure coupling. Risk: low — must re-verify no import cycle is introduced by hoisting (run the test suite / import the package).
suggested_fix: Attempt to hoist the import to module top. If `python -c 'import knowledge_base.extraction'` still succeeds, keep it hoisted; otherwise add an explicit `# deferred to avoid circular import with papers` comment at the function-local import.
references: PEP 8 (imports at top of module); Python anti-patterns: function-local imports

title: Underscore kwarg _prefetched_chunks leaks an internal optimization into a public signature
description: Current state: public `extract_structure` exposes keyword-only `_prefetched_chunks: list[dict] | None = None` (line 1059). A leading-underscore parameter on a public, `__all__`-exported function puts an implementation detail (avoid a re-query of chunks already fetched by the ETA step) into the public contract. Target state: either (a) accept `chunks: list[dict] | None = None` as a documented, properly-named optional argument, or (b) split the chunk-loading from the extraction so the caller passes already-loaded chunks to an inner function while the public API stays clean. Pattern: clarify API contract / hide implementation detail. Motivation: underscore-named public parameters are a code smell — callers cannot tell whether they may use it; docstring does not mention it. Estimated scope: rename + docstring, update the tool-layer caller. Risk: low-medium — it is referenced by the tool layer; changing the name touches that call site.
suggested_fix: Rename to a documented `chunks: list[dict] | None = None` (or factor a `_extract_with_chunks` inner), update the docstring to describe it, and adjust the operations/extraction route caller.

title: source provenance uses bare string literals instead of an Enum
description: Current state: provenance is a free `source: str = "user"` parameter on `_record_entity`, `record_method`, `record_dataset`, `record_metric`, with the literal `"llm_extraction"` threaded through internal writers (619, 654-726, 794-858) and `"user"` as the default. There is no single source of truth for the allowed values; a typo (`"llm_extration"`) would silently persist. Target state: a `class EntitySource(str, enum.Enum): USER = "user"; LLM_EXTRACTION = "llm_extraction"` and type the parameters as `EntitySource` (str-Enum keeps DB compatibility). Pattern: replace string constant with Enum (enum.Enum over string constants, per the Pythonic lens). Motivation: typo-proof, discoverable, single definition. Estimated scope: define enum, update 4 public function signatures and all internal call sites. Prerequisites: none. Risk: medium (not light) — `record_method`/`record_dataset`/`record_metric` are public, `__all__`-exported API; changing the parameter type affects external callers and tests, so it is a HEAVY change despite being mechanical-looking.
suggested_fix: Introduce `class EntitySource(str, Enum)` with `USER`/`LLM_EXTRACTION`; change `source` params to accept `EntitySource` (str subclass preserves SQLite binding); replace literal `"llm_extraction"`/`"user"` usages with the enum members.
references: PEP 435 (enum); Effective Python: prefer enums for sets of related constants

title: Magic number 5 for ETA logging cadence (and inline /60 seconds-to-minutes)
description: Current state: `if completed_count % 5 == 0 or completed_count == len(chunks):` uses an unnamed `5` to throttle ETA log lines, and the same block divides by `60` inline to convert seconds to minutes (`remaining / 60`). These are unexplained constants. (Note: `AVG_SECONDS_PER_CHUNK = 4` is already a named module-level constant in `__all__`, so it is NOT a magic number.) Target state: a module-level `_ETA_LOG_EVERY_N_CHUNKS = 5` named constant; the `/60` is a well-understood unit conversion and is acceptable but could be a `_SECONDS_PER_MINUTE = 60` for clarity. Pattern: replace magic number with named constant. Motivation: self-documenting logging cadence. Estimated scope: one constant + one reference. Risk: none.
suggested_fix: Add `_ETA_LOG_EVERY_N_CHUNKS = 5` near the other module constants and use it in the modulo check; optionally name the 60-second conversion.
references: Clean Code: replace magic numbers with named constants

title: _EXTRACT_PROMPT and _MAP_PROMPT are near-identical templates
description: Current state: `_EXTRACT_PROMPT` (405-418) and `_MAP_PROMPT` (421-437) share the same three-array JSON spec and trailing instructions; `_MAP_PROMPT` only adds the `({chunk_index}/{total_chunks})` header and the `surface_forms` field in the methods/datasets schemas. The shared body is copy-pasted. Target state: a common body fragment composed with the two variants, OR a single parameterized template builder. Pattern: Extract Template / parameterize prompt. Motivation: keeps the two extraction schemas in sync (a change to the metrics schema currently must be made twice). Estimated scope: small, local. Prerequisites: none. Risk: medium and explicitly NOT light — prompt text is behavior-affecting for the LLM; any whitespace/wording drift during extraction can change extraction quality. Must be validated against the slow/real-LLM tests, not just unit tests.
suggested_fix: Factor the shared spec into a constant fragment and build both prompts from it via `.format`/composition, preserving exact wording. Validate with the `@pytest.mark.slow` extraction tests before merging.

title: extract_structure 'Paper not found' raise path untested
description: extract_structure raises NotFoundError(f"Paper {paper_id} not found") when the SELECT id FROM papers returns no row (line 1069-1071). The existing test suite only exercises the sibling 'No chunks found' branch (tests/test_extraction.py:208-213, test_extract_structure_no_chunks). No test passes a paper_id that does not exist in the papers table, so the first NotFoundError branch and its message are unverified. The same untested 'Paper not found' branch exists in estimate_extraction_time (extraction.py:1033-1035).
suggested_fix: Add a test that calls extract_structure(conn, 99999) on a fresh DB with no such paper and asserts pytest.raises(NotFoundError, match="Paper 99999 not found"). Add the analogous test for estimate_extraction_time.

title: estimate_extraction_time short-doc and no-chunks branches untested
description: estimate_extraction_time has three observable behaviors: NotFoundError on missing paper (1033-1035), NotFoundError on no chunks (1037-1039), and a return dict whose is_long flag is total_chars > SINGLE_PASS_CHAR_LIMIT (1047). The only test, test_estimate_extraction_time_long_doc (tests/test_extraction.py:448-463), asserts is_long is True for a large doc. The short-doc branch (is_long is False) and both NotFoundError raises are never asserted, and the returned total_chars / estimated_seconds / chunks values are not checked against expected arithmetic (estimated_seconds == chunk_count * AVG_SECONDS_PER_CHUNK).
suggested_fix: Add test_estimate_extraction_time_short_doc asserting is_long is False and estimated_seconds == chunk_count * AVG_SECONDS_PER_CHUNK for a small document; add raises tests for the missing-paper and no-chunks paths.

title: compare_papers <2 paper_ids early-return untested
description: compare_papers returns [] immediately when len(paper_ids) < 2 (lines 352-353). Both existing tests (tests/test_extraction.py:116, 142) pass exactly two paper_ids. There is no call with an empty list or a single-element list, so the early-return guard and its boundary (exactly 2 vs fewer) are never exercised. A regression that removes the guard would still pass the current suite.
suggested_fix: Parametrize a test over paper_ids in ([], [p1]) asserting compare_papers returns []. This documents the boundary and protects the guard.

title: _record_entity invalid-table ValueError guard untested
description: _record_entity raises ValueError(f"Invalid entity table: {table!r}") when table is not in _ENTITY_TABLES (lines 193-194). This is a security-relevant guard since `table` is f-string-interpolated directly into the INSERT/SELECT SQL (lines 195-206); the frozenset allowlist is the only barrier against SQL injection via table name. No test passes an out-of-allowlist table value, so the guard is unverified. record_method/record_dataset are thin wrappers that always pass a literal valid table, so the guard is never reached through them.
suggested_fix: Add a test that calls _record_entity(conn, "metrics", ...) or "papers; DROP" and asserts pytest.raises(ValueError, match="Invalid entity table"). This pins the injection-prevention contract.

title: _sanitize_str whitespace-retention behavior untested
description: _sanitize_str strips control chars via _CONTROL_CHAR_RE but the regex deliberately excludes \t (0x09), \n (0x0a), \r (0x0d) from removal (comment at line 55, pattern at line 56). The only indirect coverage is test_strips_control_characters (tests/test_extraction.py:1767-1777) which checks \x00 and \x01 removal on a name. There is no assertion that tabs/newlines/carriage-returns are RETAINED, nor a direct unit test of _sanitize_str, nor a test that truncation to max_len occurs after control-char stripping. A regression broadening the regex to strip \n would silently corrupt descriptions and pass the suite.
suggested_fix: Add a direct test on _sanitize_str: assert _sanitize_str("a\tb\nc\r", 100) == "a\tb\nc\r" (whitespace kept) and assert _sanitize_str("x\x07y", 100) == "xy" (other control chars dropped); add a truncation-after-strip case.

title: _store_resolved llm_extraction provenance not asserted
description: _store_resolved hardcodes source='llm_extraction' when inserting into entities (line 619) and passes source='llm_extraction' to record_method/record_dataset/record_metric (lines 661, 673, 725). TestProvenanceColumn::test_extract_single_pass_sets_llm_source (tests/test_extraction.py:2014-2070) verifies this only for the single-pass path. The map-reduce store path (_store_resolved) has several tests (e.g. test_store_resolved_writes_entities_and_methods:344) but none asserts the source column equals 'llm_extraction' on entities/methods/datasets/metrics. Provenance is a documented feature (Data Fidelity / source taxonomy) yet the map-reduce branch's provenance is unguarded.
suggested_fix: Extend an existing _store_resolved test (or add one) to SELECT source FROM entities/methods/datasets/metrics WHERE paper_id = ? and assert each is 'llm_extraction'.

title: get_entities empty-paper and confidence passthrough untested
description: get_entities returns [] for a paper with no entities and surfaces mentions[].confidence from entity_mentions (line 1098, 1111). test_get_entities (tests/test_extraction.py:515-540) covers exactly one entity with one mention and asserts surface_form but never the confidence value, and there is no test for the empty-result case (paper with zero entities). The confidence column is selected and returned but its value is never asserted, so a column-aliasing regression would be invisible.
suggested_fix: Add test_get_entities_empty asserting get_entities(conn, paper_with_no_entities) == []; extend test_get_entities to assert entities[0]['mentions'][0]['confidence'] == 0.9 (the inserted value).

title: Metric value float-coercion of numeric strings/ints untested
description: _validate_extraction coerces metric value via float(value) and accepts numeric strings/ints, rejecting only non-finite and non-numeric (lines 117-122). Tests cover rejection of value='high' (test:1819), NaN (test:1842), and inf (test:1853), and acceptance of float literals, but there is no test that value='0.95' (numeric STRING) or value=88 (int) is accepted and coerced to float. The single-pass and map-reduce store paths also independently call float(value) (lines 843, 701); the string-number acceptance contract is load-bearing because LLMs frequently return values as JSON strings, yet it is unasserted at the validation layer.
suggested_fix: Add a parametrized test over value in ('0.95', 88, 88.5) asserting _validate_extraction yields one metric whose value is a float and isinstance(value, float).

title: estimate_extraction_time docstring contradicts actual error behavior and omits returned key
description: The docstring states 'Returns {"error": ...} if paper or chunks not found' (line 1031), but the function does not return an error dict on those conditions — it raises NotFoundError (line 1035 for missing paper, line 1039 for missing chunks). The error-dict contract is stale and contradicts the code; a caller reading the docstring would write a branch checking for an 'error' key that never appears, while an uncaught NotFoundError propagates. Additionally, the docstring lists the return keys as 'total_chars, chunk_count, estimated_seconds, is_long' (line 1030) but the actual return dict (lines 1043-1049) also includes a 'chunks' key, which is undocumented.
suggested_fix: Update the docstring to document the Raises (NotFoundError when paper or chunks are absent) instead of the nonexistent error-dict return, and add the 'chunks' key to the documented return shape. E.g.: 'Returns dict with total_chars, chunk_count, estimated_seconds, is_long, chunks. Raises NotFoundError if the paper or its chunks do not exist.'

title: Public exported functions get_methods/get_datasets/get_metrics lack docstrings
description: get_methods (line 284), get_datasets (line 301), and get_metrics (line 318) are all listed in __all__ (lines 37-39), making them public API, yet none has a docstring. By contrast their sibling public functions record_method (line 222), record_dataset (line 246), record_metric (line 273), and get_entities (line 1089) all carry at least a one-line summary docstring. This is an inconsistency in docstring presence across the public surface of the same module.
suggested_fix: Add a one-line summary docstring to each, matching the existing minimalist style, e.g. get_methods: '"""List recorded methods for a paper."""', get_datasets: '"""List recorded datasets for a paper."""', get_metrics: '"""List recorded metrics for a paper, joined with method/dataset names."""'.

title: extract_structure docstring does not document its parameters or unused 'confirmed' arg
description: extract_structure has five parameters beyond conn/paper_id: confirmed (line 1055), on_progress (line 1056), max_workers (line 1058), and _prefetched_chunks (line 1059). The docstring (lines 1061-1068) only mentions max_workers in prose and gives no parameter section. More notably, the 'confirmed' parameter is accepted but never referenced anywhere in the function body (verified: the only occurrence of 'confirmed' in the module is the signature at line 1055). The docstring's note that 'The caller (tool layer) is responsible for ETA confirmation flow' hints at why it exists but does not document that the argument is inert here, so a reader cannot tell that passing confirmed=False vs True has no effect on this function. The return-dict shape is also undocumented despite differing between the single-pass and map-reduce paths.
suggested_fix: Document the parameters explicitly. State that 'confirmed' is accepted for interface symmetry with the tool layer but is not consulted here (or remove it if the tool layer can drop it). Document on_progress, _prefetched_chunks, and the differing return shapes of the single-pass vs map-reduce branches.

title: Per-row embed_spaces.chunk_count UPDATE in insert_chunk_vec causes N+1 writes during bulk ingestion
description: insert_chunk_vec() fires a second statement `UPDATE embed_spaces SET chunk_count = chunk_count + 1 WHERE table_name = ? AND status = 'active'` on every single call, right after the vec INSERT. The sole caller (src/knowledge_base/ingest.py:180) invokes insert_chunk_vec once per chunk inside the per-chunk ingestion path, so a document producing N chunks generates N redundant UPDATE statements against embed_spaces. Each UPDATE is a separate round-trip and a table scan/filter on (table_name, status). The chunk_count is pure bookkeeping derived from row inserts; it does not need to be incremented one-at-a-time. This is the classic N+1 write amplification pattern and is the dominant per-chunk overhead beyond the actual vector insert. The symmetric delete_chunk_vecs() already does a single batched count+decrement, showing the bulk-aware pattern is intended.
suggested_fix: Decouple the count bookkeeping from the per-row insert: have insert_chunk_vec only do the vec INSERT, and add a separate `bump_chunk_count(conn, table_name, delta)` (or a bulk insert_chunk_vecs(...)) that the ingestion loop calls once after inserting all chunks for a document (delta = number inserted). Alternatively, drop the redundant counter entirely and compute chunk_count on demand via `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [tbl]` when reporting status, since it is denormalized state that can drift. If keeping the per-call API for compatibility, add a `bump_count: bool = True` parameter so bulk callers can pass bump_count=False and update once.

title: delete_chunk_vecs runs a separate COUNT pass before DELETE instead of using cursor.rowcount
description: delete_chunk_vecs() executes a full batched SELECT COUNT(*) pass over the vec table (via _batched_select) purely to learn how many rows will be removed, then runs a second batched DELETE over the same id set (via _batched_execute). This is two full passes over the same WHERE chunk_id IN (...) predicate when one would suffice: SQLite's DELETE returns the affected-row count in cursor.rowcount, which would yield the identical `actual_deleted` value without the extra round-trips. The double pass doubles the number of SQL statements and predicate evaluations for every chunk deletion. _batched_execute currently discards the cursor (line 88), so it cannot surface rowcount as written.
suggested_fix: Make _batched_execute accumulate and return the sum of `conn.execute(...).rowcount` across batches (it already issues the executes), then have delete_chunk_vecs use that return value as actual_deleted and drop the _batched_select count pass entirely. cursor.rowcount is reliable for DELETE in sqlite3 (no SELECT precedes it). This halves the statement count for deletions and removes the now-unnecessary COUNT query.

title: Dynamic SQL table-name interpolation in vec helpers relies on non-local sanitization invariant
description: insert_chunk_vec (L438), delete_chunk_vecs (L463, L466) and the SELECT/DELETE templates passed to _batched_select/_batched_execute interpolate the vec table name directly into SQL via f-strings: f"INSERT INTO [{tbl}] ...", f"SELECT COUNT(*) ... FROM [{tbl}] WHERE chunk_id IN ({{ph}})", and f"DELETE FROM [{tbl}] WHERE ...". SQLite cannot bind identifiers as parameters, so this f-string form is structurally necessary. The values are safe TODAY because every table name originates from get_vec_table_name() (returning the literal 'chunks_vec' or a name read from embed_spaces.table_name) and the names stored there are constrained to [a-zA-Z0-9_] by space_table_name() (re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9_]','_',name), L375) and the _SPACE_NAME_RE.match() guard in embed_swap.create_space (L70). However, the safety of these f-strings is a non-local invariant: it depends entirely on (a) every future caller routing space creation through create_space rather than calling space_table_name + INSERT directly, and (b) the embed_spaces.table_name column never being written with an unvalidated value. delete_chunk_vecs/insert_chunk_vec do NOT re-validate the name they read back from the DB. A tampered DB row, a future code path that inserts a space name without the regex guard, or a refactor that drops the guard would reopen a first-class SQL-injection vector (CWE-89) since the bracketed identifier can be broken out of with a ']' character. This is defense-in-depth hardening, not an exploitable bug in the current call graph.
suggested_fix: Centralize identifier validation at the point of use rather than trusting upstream. Add a guard in db.py that asserts the table name matches a strict allowlist before any f-string interpolation, e.g. a module-level _VEC_TABLE_RE = re.compile(r'^chunks_vec(_[A-Za-z0-9_]+)?$') and a helper _safe_vec_table(tbl) that raises ValueError on mismatch; call it at the top of insert_chunk_vec, delete_chunk_vecs (before building the COUNT/DELETE templates), and anywhere get_vec_table_name() feeds an f-string. This makes the SQL-injection-safety invariant local and enforced at the trust boundary, independent of upstream callers.

title: init_schema is a ~300 LOC god function mixing DDL, seeding, and migration orchestration
description: Current state: init_schema() runs ~300 lines spanning four distinct responsibilities: (1) config table creation + default seeding (597-621), (2) a giant executescript() DDL block creating all ~16 tables/vtables/triggers (623-843), (3) index creation and config INSERT-OR-IGNORE seeding (845-877), and (4) sequential invocation of 14 migration steps (881-896). It far exceeds the 50 LOC smell threshold and mixes abstraction levels (raw SQL string templating next to high-level migration sequencing). Target state: extract a `_create_base_schema(conn, embed_dim)` for the DDL block, a `_seed_default_config(conn)` for config seeding (currently split awkwardly across 607-615 and 855-871), and a `_run_migrations(conn, embed_dim)` driving an ordered list/registry of migration callables; init_schema becomes a ~15 LOC orchestrator. Pattern name: Extract Method / Replace Method-with-Method-Object; migration registry is a Collecting Parameter / pipeline pattern. Motivation: testability (each phase independently testable), readability, and reduced merge-conflict surface as schema grows. Estimated scope: single file, ~5 new private functions, no behavior change. Prerequisites: none (all logic already private to db.py). Risk: LOW-MEDIUM — ordering of migration calls is load-bearing (e.g. _migrate_session_id must precede _migrate_chunk_sessions backfill, _bootstrap_embed_spaces sits between specific migrations); the extraction must preserve exact call order. Recommend asserting current order via a test before refactor.
suggested_fix: Split init_schema into _create_base_schema, _seed_default_config, and a _run_migrations driver iterating an explicit ordered tuple of migration functions; keep init_schema as a thin orchestrator. Preserve exact migration ordering and add a regression test pinning it.

title: Eleven _migrate_* functions duplicate the same 'introspect-then-alter/rebuild' preamble
description: Current state: the 11 migration helpers fall into two repeated shapes. Shape A (add-column): _migrate_session_id (267-273), _migrate_chunk_strategy (276-284), _migrate_embed_spaces_matryoshka (496-504), _migrate_embed_spaces_element_type (507-518), _migrate_extraction_source (539-549) all run `columns = {row[1] for row in conn.execute('PRAGMA table_info(<t>)').fetchall()}`, guard on membership, then ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN + commit. Shape B (table-rebuild): _migrate_source_type_figure (165-217), _migrate_relationship_types (220-248), _migrate_jobs_types (303-337) all read `SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master ... name='<t>'`, substring-check the CHECK constraint, then run a PRAGMA foreign_keys=OFF / CREATE _new / INSERT SELECT / DROP / RENAME / foreign_keys=ON script. Target state: two reusable helpers — `_add_column_if_missing(conn, table, name, ddl)` and `_rebuild_table_if(conn, table, sentinel, new_ddl, copy_cols=None)` — collapsing each migration to a 1-3 line call. Pattern name: Form Template Method / Extract Helper. Motivation: removes ~120 lines of duplicated introspection logic and centralizes the brittle table-rebuild dance (the FK-off/commit/FK-on sequence is currently copy-pasted three times and easy to get subtly wrong). Estimated scope: single file, 2 helpers + 11 call-site rewrites. Prerequisites: none. Risk: MEDIUM — table-rebuild migrations have per-case column-mapping nuances (e.g. _migrate_source_type_figure dynamically builds old_cols/new_cols for very old schemas at 172-182); the helper must support an explicit column list rather than assuming `INSERT INTO new SELECT *`. Do not over-generalize; Shape A is the safe LIGHT subset.
suggested_fix: Introduce _add_column_if_missing(conn, table, column, type_clause) and refactor the five add-column migrations to use it (LIGHT). Optionally introduce a guarded table-rebuild helper for Shape B with an explicit column-list parameter (HEAVY).

title: co_occurrence_pairs and get_active_space return untyped dicts where a dataclass/TypedDict belongs
description: Current state: co_occurrence_pairs returns `list[dict]` (117) building dicts with hardcoded keys source_uri_a/source_uri_b/co_sessions (143-149); get_active_space returns `dict | None` (379) via `dict(row)`. Callers (embed_swap.py:280/420, routes/embeddings.py:31/284, folder_summaries.py:114, ingest.py:91, search.py:270/274) then index these dicts by string key with no static guarantee the key exists or is typed. This is primitive obsession: the embed_spaces row has a fixed, known shape (name, model, provider, dim, chunk_strategy, status, table_name, chunk_count, total_chunks, matryoshka_base_dim, element_type) that would be far better expressed as a frozen dataclass or TypedDict. Target state: define `@dataclass(frozen=True) class EmbedSpace` (or a `TypedDict`) and `class CoOccurrencePair` and return those; pyright then validates field access at all call sites. Pattern name: Replace Data Value with Object / Introduce Parameter Object. Motivation: type safety across 6 call sites, self-documenting return contract, IDE autocomplete, and resistance to schema drift. Estimated scope: db.py + ~6 call-site files (mechanical key->attribute rename). Prerequisites: agree on dataclass vs TypedDict (TypedDict is the lower-churn choice since sqlite3.Row already supports mapping access and dict() conversion). Risk: MEDIUM — touches multiple modules; any JSON serialization of these dicts (e.g. routes returning json.dumps) must be handled (dataclasses.asdict).
suggested_fix: Introduce an EmbedSpace TypedDict (or frozen dataclass) for get_active_space and a CoOccurrencePair TypedDict for co_occurrence_pairs; annotate return types and update call sites to typed access.

title: get_active_chunk_strategy is unused dead code (no external callers, absent from __all__)
description: Current state: get_active_chunk_strategy (397-404) is a module-level public function but has zero callers outside db.py (verified via grep across src/knowledge_base/*.py) and is NOT listed in __all__ (16-33). It is effectively dead code with an inconsistent visibility signal: public name, no export, no use. Its sibling get_active_space/get_vec_table_name/get_space_element_type are all exported and used. Target state: delete it, or if it is intended future API, add it to __all__ and document the intended consumer. Pattern name: Remove Dead Code. Motivation: reduces API-surface confusion and maintenance burden; a public name that nothing calls invites accidental divergence from the active-space contract. Estimated scope: single function deletion (8 lines). Prerequisites: confirm no dynamic/getattr access (none found). Risk: LOW.
suggested_fix: Delete get_active_chunk_strategy, or promote it to __all__ if a consumer is planned. Confirmed no callers in the package today.

title: _SPACE_NAME_RE is underscore-prefixed but imported cross-module by embed_swap.py
description: Current state: _SPACE_NAME_RE (370) carries the leading-underscore 'module-private' convention yet is imported by embed_swap.py:14 and used at embed_swap.py:70 for space-name validation. A private name consumed across module boundaries sends contradictory signals: PEP 8 marks it internal, but the codebase treats it as shared API. It is also not in __all__. Target state: either (a) promote it to a public name like SPACE_NAME_RE and add to __all__ since it is genuinely shared validation, or (b) expose the validation behind a public predicate `is_valid_space_name(name) -> bool` and keep the regex truly private. Option (b) is cleaner — it hides the regex implementation and gives a typed, documented contract. Pattern name: Encapsulate Field / Hide Delegate. Motivation: removes the private-name leak and decouples embed_swap.py from db.py's regex representation. Estimated scope: db.py + 1 call site in embed_swap.py. Prerequisites: none. Risk: LOW.
suggested_fix: Add `def is_valid_space_name(name: str) -> bool: return bool(_SPACE_NAME_RE.match(name))`, export it in __all__, and have embed_swap.py call it instead of importing the private regex. Alternatively rename to SPACE_NAME_RE and export.

title: CHECK constraint definitions are duplicated as literal strings between schema DDL and migrations
description: Current state: the relationships CHECK is generated by _relationship_check_constraint() at init (676) but hardcoded as a literal `relation_type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(relation_type IN ('extends', ... 'similar'))` inside _migrate_relationship_types (235) — the migration does not reuse the helper, so adding a relationship type requires editing RELATIONSHIP_TYPES AND remembering the migration's literal will only fire on the substring `'similar'` (224). Likewise the jobs job_type CHECK list lives both in the init DDL (783) and verbatim in _migrate_jobs_types (317), keyed off the substring `'auto_relate'`. The source_type CHECK appears at 191 and 628. Target state: derive all rebuild-migration DDL CHECK clauses from the same constants/helpers used by init_schema (e.g. _relationship_check_constraint already exists; introduce JOB_TYPES / SOURCE_TYPES tuples + helpers). Pattern name: Single Source of Truth / Extract Constant. Motivation: prevents schema/migration drift — today a new enum value silently fails to propagate to the rebuild path. Estimated scope: single file, 3 migration DDL blocks + 2 new constant tuples. Prerequisites: none. Risk: LOW-MEDIUM — the rebuild migrations also depend on the column ordering of INSERT ... SELECT * (242, 329); only the CHECK string is being de-duplicated, leave the INSERT untouched.
suggested_fix: Add JOB_TYPES and SOURCE_TYPES tuples with corresponding _check_constraint helpers; reference _relationship_check_constraint() inside _migrate_relationship_types and the new helpers inside the jobs/chunks rebuild migrations so the enum lists have one definition.

title: get_connection uses an unnamed magic timeout of 30.0 seconds
description: Current state: get_connection hardcodes `timeout=30.0` (155) with no named constant or comment explaining the value. SQLite's connect timeout governs busy-handler wait under WAL contention — a meaningful, tunable knob that reads as an arbitrary literal here. Target state: hoist to a module constant `_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 30.0` with a one-line rationale (WAL lock-wait budget). Pattern name: Replace Magic Number with Symbolic Constant. Motivation: discoverability and single point of tuning; mirrors the good practice already applied to _SQL_BATCH_SIZE (67-69), which is named and documented. Estimated scope: 2 lines. Prerequisites: none. Risk: LOW.
suggested_fix: Define `_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 30.0  # WAL busy-wait budget` near _SQL_BATCH_SIZE and reference it in sqlite3.connect.

title: Active-space table_name fallback (`tbl = table_name or get_vec_table_name(conn)`) repeated across three vec helpers
description: Current state: insert_chunk_vec (433), delete_chunk_vecs (460), and delete_chunks_cascade->delete_chunk_vecs all repeat the `tbl = table_name or get_vec_table_name(conn)` resolution, and insert_chunk_vec additionally re-resolves element_type (434-435). The trio also independently issues the embed_spaces.chunk_count +/- bookkeeping UPDATEs (442-446, 469-473), making the count-maintenance invariant spread across functions. Target state: a small `_resolve_vec_table(conn, table_name) -> str` helper removes the fallback duplication; optionally a `_adjust_chunk_count(conn, table_name, delta)` centralizes the bookkeeping so the +1/-N invariant lives in one place. Pattern name: Extract Method / Centralize Invariant. Motivation: the chunk_count bookkeeping is a correctness invariant currently maintained in two directions in two functions — a single helper makes drift less likely. Estimated scope: single file, 1-2 helpers + 3 call sites. Prerequisites: none. Risk: LOW.
suggested_fix: Extract `_resolve_vec_table(conn, table_name)` and (optionally) `_adjust_chunk_count(conn, table_name, delta)`; route the three vec helpers through them.

title: escape_like has zero tests despite 5+ production LIKE-clause call sites
description: escape_like() escapes backslash, percent, and underscore for SQLite LIKE clauses (ESCAPE '\\'). It is imported by conclusions.py:69, folder_summaries.py:27, bibtex.py:106, and papers.py:234 — every wildcard search in the codebase funnels through it. No test file references escape_like (confirmed: grep across tests/ returns 0 hits). The function is pure, order-sensitive (backslash MUST be escaped first or the %/_ escapes get double-escaped), and a regression here silently breaks search correctness or opens a LIKE-injection where a user keyword containing % matches everything. The ordering of the three .replace() calls is exactly the kind of subtle invariant that needs a guard test.
suggested_fix: Add tests/test_db.py cases covering: no special chars (identity), a lone '%' -> '\\%', a lone '_' -> '\\_', a lone '\\' -> '\\\\', and a combined input like 'a_b%c\\d' to lock the backslash-first ordering. Optionally add a hypothesis property: for any string s, escaping then using it in a LIKE ... ESCAPE '\\' against the literal s matches exactly that row in an in-memory SQLite table (round-trip invariant).

title: delete_chunk_vecs has no direct unit test for empty-input, chunk_count decrement, or explicit table
description: delete_chunk_vecs() is exported in __all__ and called from web.py (lines 843, 984, 1046) and indirectly via delete_chunks_cascade. No test references it directly (grep tests/ = 0). Three behaviours are uncovered: (1) the empty-list early return at line 458-459 (`if not chunk_ids: return`); (2) the chunk_count bookkeeping at 468-473 — it counts ACTUAL rows present before deleting (count_rows) and decrements embed_spaces.chunk_count with a MAX(0, ...) clamp, only when actual_deleted>0. Nothing asserts that count stays correct when some chunk_ids have no embedding, nor that the clamp prevents going negative; (3) the table_name override path. The batching boundary is exercised only indirectly through reingest (test_ingest.py:753 patches _SQL_BATCH_SIZE=2) but that test asserts reingest outcomes, not this function's chunk_count contract.
suggested_fix: Add direct tests: (a) delete_chunk_vecs(conn, []) is a no-op and leaves chunk_count unchanged; (b) insert N vecs into the active space, delete a subset, assert embed_spaces.chunk_count decremented by exactly the number of rows that actually existed (mix in chunk_ids with no embedding to verify count uses actual rows); (c) verify MAX(0,...) clamp by deleting more than chunk_count and asserting count floors at 0; (d) delete from an explicit non-active table_name. Parametrize across element_type float32/int8.

title: delete_chunks_cascade lacks direct test for return value and empty-list path
description: delete_chunks_cascade() (exported, called from vision.py:1986, web.py:515, ingest.py:576) has no direct unit test (grep tests/ = 0). Uncovered: (1) the empty-list early return returning 0 (line 489-490); (2) the documented return value `len(chunk_ids)` — note this is the INPUT count, not the number of rows actually deleted, so calling it with non-existent ids returns a non-zero count while deleting nothing; that off-by-semantics is a real trap worth pinning with a test; (3) the two-step cascade ordering (vec rows before chunk rows so the chunks_ad FTS trigger fires correctly) and that the FTS index is cleaned up after cascade.
