suggested_fix: Add tests: (a) delete_chunks_cascade(conn, []) returns 0 and mutates nothing; (b) insert chunks + vecs + an FTS-indexed body, call cascade, assert return == len(input), assert chunks/chunks_vec rows gone AND chunks_fts no longer MATCHes the deleted content; (c) pin the return-count semantics by passing one real id and one bogus id and asserting return==2 while only one row was actually removed (documents the contract or surfaces it as a bug to the reviewer).

title: insert_chunk_vec chunk_count increment side-effect is never asserted
description: test_embed_spaces.py exercises insert_chunk_vec for row insertion (test_insert_chunk_vec_helper at line 359, test_insert_chunk_vec_explicit_table at 372, test_insert_chunk_vec_auto_resolves_element_type at 567), but none assert the second side-effect at lines 442-446: incrementing embed_spaces.chunk_count for the active space matching table_name. The UPDATE is gated on `status = 'active'`, so inserting into a non-active explicit table (the test_insert_chunk_vec_explicit_table case targets a 'populating' space) must NOT bump the active default's count — an easy-to-break invariant that keeps backfill bookkeeping honest. delete_chunk_vecs decrements unconditionally by table_name (no status filter), so insert/delete are asymmetric on the status condition; only a test pins this.
suggested_fix: Extend the existing insert_chunk_vec tests: after insert into the active 'default' space, assert embed_spaces.chunk_count incremented by 1; after insert into a non-active explicit table, assert the active space's chunk_count is UNCHANGED (locks the status='active' gate). Pair with a delete to show the round-trip returns chunk_count to its starting value.

title: Table-rebuild legacy migrations have no legacy-path tests (only the no-op fresh-DB path runs)
description: Several migrations perform real data transforms only on legacy schemas, but tests only ever call init_schema on a FRESH DB where these short-circuit at the guard (e.g. `if not row or \'figure\' in row[0]: return`). No test constructs the old schema to exercise the transform body. Untested transform bodies: _migrate_source_type_figure (165-217, rebuilds chunks adding 'figure' to the source_type CHECK + re-creates the 3 FTS triggers and the INSERT INTO ... SELECT with conditional session_id/chunk_strategy columns); _migrate_relationship_types (220-248, rebuilds relationships to add 'similar'); _migrate_jobs_types (303-337, rebuilds jobs to add 'auto_relate' and re-creates idx_jobs_status_created); _migrate_papers_fts (251-264, backfill guarded on empty FTS + non-empty papers); _migrate_extraction_source (539-549, adds 'source' col to 4 tables). By contrast _migrate_session_id, _migrate_chunk_strategy, _migrate_chunk_sessions, and _migrate_normalize_source_uri DO have legacy-path tests — so the pattern exists and just needs extending. The riskiest is _migrate_source_type_figure: a column-list mismatch in INSERT INTO chunks_new SELECT silently drops/misaligns data and is invisible on fresh DBs.
suggested_fix: Add one legacy-path test per transform mirroring test_chunk_strategy_migration / test_migrate_chunk_sessions_backfill: build the pre-migration schema (old CHECK constraint, no 'figure'/'similar'/'auto_relate'), insert representative rows, run init_schema, then assert (a) the new constraint value is accepted, (b) every pre-existing row survived with correct column values, (c) for source_type_figure that the FTS triggers still fire after the table swap, (d) for jobs that idx_jobs_status_created exists post-migration. For _migrate_papers_fts, create papers + empty papers_fts and assert the backfill populates it (and is skipped when FTS already has rows).

title: _bootstrap_embed_spaces legacy branch (non-empty chunks_vec) and config-driven model/provider are untested
description: _bootstrap_embed_spaces registers the 'default' space. The fresh-DB path (count=0, model/provider from defaults) is implicitly covered by every _setup() in test_embed_spaces.py, but two branches are not: (1) the legacy path where chunks_vec already has rows — line 574 COUNT(*) should set chunk_count to the existing embedding count, and the comment at 578-584 explains why strategy is forced to 'mechanical' regardless of config.chunk_strategy; nothing asserts the count is propagated or that strategy stays 'mechanical' even when config says 'semantic'; (2) the model/provider override read from the config table (lines 562-570) vs the DEFAULT_* fallback when config rows are absent (the sqlite3.OperationalError fallback at 575-576 when chunks_vec doesn't exist is also unexercised).
suggested_fix: Add tests: (a) pre-populate chunks_vec with K embeddings and config.chunk_strategy='semantic', run _bootstrap_embed_spaces, assert the default space has chunk_count==K and chunk_strategy=='mechanical'; (b) set config embed_model/embed_provider to custom values and assert the bootstrapped space picks them up; (c) call on a DB lacking chunks_vec and assert count falls back to 0 without raising. Guard idempotency: a second call is a no-op (existing-space early return at 558-560).

title: co_occurrence_pairs untested for min_sessions=0 boundary and alphabetical-ordering invariant
description: co_occurrence_pairs is well tested for basic pairing, min_sessions=1/2, null sessions, and join-table sourcing (test_db.py:139-497). Two edge cases remain: (1) min_sessions=0 — HAVING co_sessions >= 0 would include every co-occurring pair (default is 1); the 0 boundary is never exercised though it is a reachable caller value; (2) the `a.source_uri < b.source_uri` ordering invariant (line 136) which guarantees source_uri_a is alphabetically first and dedups the symmetric pair — existing tests use a.md/b.md/c.md which happen to already be sorted, so a test inserting docs in reverse-alphabetical URI order (e.g. z.md ingested before a.md) is needed to actually prove the ordering rather than coincidentally satisfy it.
suggested_fix: Add parametrized cases: (a) min_sessions=0 returns the same pairs as min_sessions=1 (the < join already excludes self-pairs, so 0 and 1 coincide unless a pair shares 0 sessions which is impossible) — assert this explicitly to document intent; (b) insert two docs whose URIs sort in reverse insertion order and assert source_uri_a < source_uri_b in the result, and that the pair appears exactly once (not twice).

title: Exported public function get_connection has no docstring
description: get_connection is part of the module's public API (listed in __all__ at line 27) yet carries no docstring. It performs non-obvious side effects that callers should know about: it creates the parent directory (mkdir parents=True), loads the sqlite-vec extension, sets row_factory to sqlite3.Row, and enables WAL journal mode plus foreign_keys=ON. None of this is documented. Every other non-trivial public function in the module (escape_like, co_occurrence_pairs, the space helpers, insert/delete helpers) has a docstring, so this is a style/coverage inconsistency. The db_path default (DEFAULT_DB_PATH) and the 30s timeout also go unexplained.
suggested_fix: Add a Google-style docstring documenting the db_path parameter (default DEFAULT_DB_PATH), the returned Connection's configured state (sqlite-vec loaded, Row factory, WAL mode, foreign keys ON, 30s busy timeout), and the directory-creation side effect.

title: Exported public function init_schema has no docstring
description: init_schema is exported (listed in __all__ at line 30) and is the primary schema-bootstrap entry point invoked across the codebase (_conn.py, indexer.py, jobs.py). It has no docstring despite substantial, non-obvious behavior: it seeds the config table with embed defaults, reads embed_dim from config, creates ~16 tables/FTS/vec virtual tables plus triggers and indexes, seeds additional config keys, then runs an ordered sequence of ~12 idempotent migration helpers (lines 881-896). The ordering of migrations is load-bearing (e.g. _migrate_session_id must precede _migrate_chunk_sessions which backfills from chunks.session_id) but undocumented. This is both a docstring-coverage gap and a maintainability hazard.
suggested_fix: Add a docstring stating that init_schema is idempotent (safe to call on fresh and existing DBs), summarizing the create-then-migrate flow, noting that migration ordering is significant, and naming the conn parameter contract (an open Connection from get_connection).

title: schema.md omits the embed_spaces.element_type column
description: db.py defines an element_type column on embed_spaces (lines 840-841: `element_type TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'float32' CHECK(element_type IN ('float32', 'int8'))`), also added to existing DBs via _migrate_embed_spaces_element_type (lines 507-518) and consumed by the public function get_space_element_type (lines 407-418) and insert_chunk_vec (lines 421-446). The reference schema doc docs/reference/schema.md (embed_spaces table, lines 306-318) lists every other column but is missing element_type entirely. The schema reference is stale relative to the code: a reader of the docs would not know int8 vector storage exists.
suggested_fix: Add an `element_type` row to the embed_spaces table in docs/reference/schema.md: TEXT, NOT NULL + CHECK, default `'float32'`, description 'Vector element storage type: float32 or int8 (quantized)'.

title: schema.md CHECK-constraint list for embed_spaces is incomplete
description: The embed_spaces table definition in db.py declares four CHECK constraints: chunk_strategy IN (...), status IN (...), matryoshka_base_dim IS NULL OR matryoshka_base_dim > dim (line 839), and element_type IN ('float32','int8') (line 841). The 'CHECK constraints' bullet list in docs/reference/schema.md (lines 320-323) documents only the first two, omitting the matryoshka_base_dim range check and the element_type check. The omission leaves the documented constraint set out of sync with the actual schema.
suggested_fix: Extend the CHECK-constraints bullet list in docs/reference/schema.md (lines 320-323) to add: `matryoshka_base_dim IS NULL OR matryoshka_base_dim > dim` and `element_type IN ('float32', 'int8')`.

title: source_type filtered only at final SQL fetch — can return fewer than top_k results
description: Unlike chunk_strategy, which is pre-filtered against the candidate pool before RRF/boost/rerank (line 327), source_type is applied only at the terminal SQL fetch. chunk_ids = ranked[:top_k] (line 430) selects the top_k candidates WITHOUT regard to source_type; the SQL at lines 446-450 then drops any rows whose source_type does not match (" AND source_type = ?"). The build loop (lines 457-471) skips cids missing from chunk_map. Net effect: when source_type is supplied, the function can return fewer than top_k results even when enough matching candidates existed deeper in the merged/ranked list. The 3x over-fetch (line 254) and 2x boost window (line 363) only soften the shortfall; they do not eliminate it (e.g. a query where the relevant source_type is sparse among the top_k by score). The asymmetry with chunk_strategy (which IS pre-filtered) is the tell that this is an oversight, not a deliberate design choice.
suggested_fix: Pre-filter the candidate pool by source_type the same way chunk_strategy is handled: either fold source_type into the pre-RRF filter block (lines 326-342) by adding it to the chunks JOIN/SELECT, or apply it when selecting chunk_ids so that ranked[:top_k] already contains only source_type-matching candidates. Then top_k matching results are returned whenever they exist in the over-fetched pool.

_debate.synthesis: Both models MEDIUM: source_type applied only at final SQL fetch; 3x overfetch softens but architecturally inconsistent with chunk_strategy pre-filter.

title: Reranker scores and RRF scores mixed on incomparable scales during final sort
description: When rerank=True, the cross-encoder is run only on rerank_candidates (capped at rerank_top_n, line 402-406) and only fetchable_cids get score_map[cid] = rs (line 418). Every other entry in score_map keeps its RRF score. reranker.rerank documents scores in [0, 1] (reranker.py:193), while RRF scores are 1/(RRF_K+rank+1) ≈ 0.016 at best and decreasing. The re-sort at line 422 sorts these two incompatible scales together. A reranked candidate that the cross-encoder judged a weak match (e.g. score 0.01, a legitimate value in [0,1]) is then sorted BELOW an un-reranked candidate whose RRF score is ~0.016 — even though that un-reranked candidate was excluded from reranking precisely because it ranked lower. This inverts the intended ordering: items the reranker explicitly scored can be pushed beneath items it never saw. The defect is masked whenever every reranked score happens to exceed the top RRF score (~0.016), so it surfaces intermittently on poor-match pools.
suggested_fix: Do not mix scales. Either (a) restrict the final ranked list to only the reranked (scored) candidates when rerank succeeds — drop un-reranked entries from score_map before the re-sort — or (b) order primarily by a reranked/not-reranked tier (reranked items always above un-reranked tail), or (c) normalize RRF and reranker scores into a common scale before combining. Option (a)/(b) preserves the contract that reranker order wins.

_debate.synthesis: Opus HIGH: reranker[0,1] vs RRF(~0.016) co-sort; triggers at default top_k when source_type/chunk_strategy filter set; compounds with under-fetch; default unfiltered path unaffected (Codex caveat).

title: Up to three separate full-table reads of chunks per search on the rerank path
description: On the rerank path with a source_type/chunk_strategy filter, the chunks table is read three times for overlapping id sets: line 389 (_batched_select for id, source_type, chunk_strategy to pre-filter the rerank pool), line 410 (_fetch_chunk_contents for id, content), and line 446 (final fetch for id, content, source_type, source_uri, chunk_index). The first two operate on overlapping candidate id sets and could be collapsed into one SELECT (id, content, source_type, chunk_strategy) over pool_ids, filtering in Python (which the code already does) and reusing the fetched content for the reranker — eliminating one batched round-trip per search.
suggested_fix: Merge the line-389 metadata fetch and the line-410 content fetch into a single _batched_select over pool_ids returning id, content, source_type, chunk_strategy; filter for the rerank pool in Python and pass the already-fetched content texts to rerank_fn instead of re-querying.

title: Folder-boost threshold collapses to near-zero when best folder distance is exactly 0
description: threshold = best_distance * 2 if best_distance > 0 else 1e-6 (line 166). When the best folder distance is exactly 0.0 (an exact-match folder embedding), the threshold becomes 1e-6, so the inclusion test row["distance"] <= threshold (line 169) admits only folders with distance <= 1e-6 — effectively just the exact-match folder. This is likely intentional (an exact match should not widen the boost set to everything via a 0*2=0 threshold) but the behavior is a discontinuity worth a comment: a best_distance of 1e-9 yields threshold 2e-9 while a best_distance of 0 yields 1e-6, a 1000x jump. Low risk; flagging for clarity, not as a likely live bug.
suggested_fix: Add a brief comment explaining the zero-distance branch, or use a consistent additive epsilon (e.g. threshold = best_distance * 2 + 1e-6) so the inclusion window degrades smoothly rather than discontinuously at best_distance == 0.

title: Dynamic table/expression identifier interpolated into SQL via f-string in _vec_search
description: In `_vec_search`, the SQL is assembled with an f-string that interpolates two non-parameterizable elements: the vec table identifier `[{vec_table}]` and the match expression `{query_expr}` (lines 107-108). SQLite cannot bind table names or function expressions as parameters, so f-string interpolation here is a necessary technique, and the actual user-supplied query embedding IS correctly parameterized (the `?` placeholder bound to `_serialize_f32(query_embedding)`). The safety of the interpolated identifiers depends entirely on invariants maintained outside this function: `vec_table` originates from `embed_spaces.table_name`, which is produced by `space_table_name()` in db.py:373-376 using the sanitizer `re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9_]', '_', name)` (or the hardcoded fallback `'chunks_vec'`), and `query_expr` is looked up from the fixed `ELEMENT_QUERY_EXPR` dict (utils.py:106-109) keyed by `element_type` (defaulting to `'float32'`). At the current `search()` API boundary none of these are directly attacker-controlled, so this is NOT a confirmed exploitable injection. It is a defense-in-depth/hardening concern: correctness rests on the upstream sanitizer and the dict-key lookup never being bypassed, rather than on a local, self-evident guarantee. If a future code path ever inserted a row into `embed_spaces` with an unsanitized `table_name`, or if `get_space_element_type` were changed to return an arbitrary string used as a dict key with `.get(..., default)` semantics, this f-string would become an injection sink. Recommend documenting the trust invariant at the call site and/or asserting `vec_table` matches `^chunks_vec[a-zA-Z0-9_]*$` and `element_type in VALID_ELEMENT_TYPES` defensively before interpolation.
suggested_fix: Add a defensive guard immediately before the f-string in `_vec_search`: validate that `vec_table` matches a strict allowlist pattern (e.g. `re.fullmatch(r'chunks_vec[a-zA-Z0-9_]*', vec_table)`) and that `element_type` is in `VALID_ELEMENT_TYPES` (the latter is already implicit via the dict lookup, which raises KeyError on unknown keys). Raise ValidationError on mismatch. This converts the cross-module invariant into a locally-enforced precondition and keeps the parameterized embedding binding unchanged.

title: search() is a ~270-line god function mixing 8 distinct phases
description: Current state: search() spans lines 202-473 (~200 LOC excluding docstring) and sequentially performs eight cohesive but distinct phases in one body: (1) input validation 239-252, (2) space-config resolution 256-277, (3) FTS leg 288-301, (4) vec leg 303-324, (5) pre-RRF chunk_strategy filtering 326-342, (6) merge/match-type selection 344-359, (7) folder boost windowing 361-375, (8) cross-encoder rerank 377-428, plus final fetch+build 430-473. Each phase has its own locals and abstraction level, so the reader must hold the whole pipeline in working memory. Target state: extract phases into private stage helpers (e.g. _resolve_space, _run_legs, _apply_strategy_filter, _select_merge, _apply_rerank) so search() reads as an orchestration of named steps. Pattern name: Extract Method / Compose Method (pipeline stages). Motivation: function length and mixed altitude are the module's dominant maintainability cost; the existing private helpers (_fts_search, _vec_search, _rrf_merge, _folder_boost) prove the module already favors this decomposition style — the orchestrator just stopped short. Estimated scope: single file, ~6 new private functions, no behavior change. Prerequisites: tests in tests/test_search.py covering each mode/flag combination must stay green. Risk: medium — shared mutable locals (score_map, ranked, match_type) thread across phases, so extraction must thread state explicitly to avoid subtle reordering bugs.
suggested_fix: Extract each numbered phase into a private helper that takes/returns explicit state; reduce search() to a linear sequence of helper calls. Keep the public signature unchanged.

title: Closed string vocabularies use frozensets + comments instead of enum.StrEnum
description: Current state: the closed sets mode, source_type, chunk_strategy, and match_type are modeled as bare strings: VALID_MODES/VALID_SOURCE_TYPES/VALID_CHUNK_STRATEGIES frozensets (lines 62-64) guard inputs by membership, and SearchResult.match_type is typed str with the legal values relegated to an inline comment (line 33: "# 'fts', 'vec', 'hybrid', 'reranked'"). Target state: model each closed vocabulary as an enum.StrEnum (Python 3.12+ per project, so StrEnum is available). match_type becomes a typed enum field that the type checker enforces; the frozensets become enum membership checks; the f-string error messages still format cleanly since StrEnum members are str. Pattern name: Replace Type Code with Enum (StrEnum). Motivation: this is the canonical 'enum.Enum over string constants' Pythonic smell — the comment on line 33 is documentation that the type system could enforce, and 'reranked' is a valid match_type produced only at line 469, invisible to callers reading the dataclass. Estimated scope: one file (plus call sites passing literal mode/source_type strings, which keep working under StrEnum). Risk: low — StrEnum is str-compatible, so existing string comparisons and SQL params are unaffected.
suggested_fix: Introduce SearchMode, SourceType, ChunkStrategy, MatchType as enum.StrEnum; type SearchResult.match_type: MatchType; derive the VALID_* sets from the enums or replace membership checks with enum coercion.

title: space_cfg is a free-form dict where a dataclass belongs (primitive obsession)
description: Current state: lines 261-265 build an ad-hoc dict space_cfg = {"model":..., "dim":..., "provider":...} that is then accessed by string key at lines 306-319 (space_cfg["model"], space_cfg["dim"], space_cfg["provider"]). The alternative branch (line 273) assigns space_cfg = get_embed_config(conn), so the same key contract is shared with embed_swap but is nowhere typed. Target state: a SpaceConfig dataclass (colocated with embed_swap.get_embed_config / get_space) with model/dim/provider fields; both branches return it; access becomes attribute access with type-checker support. Pattern name: Replace Data Clob / Introduce Parameter Object (dataclass). Motivation: the module already uses a dataclass for SearchResult, so the dict here is an inconsistency and a direct hit on the lens's 'dicts where dataclasses belong'; key typos (e.g. space_cfg["dimension"]) fail only at runtime. Estimated scope: touches search.py plus embed_swap config accessors that return/consume the shape. Risk: low, but crosses a module boundary so coordinate the get_embed_config return-type change.
suggested_fix: Define a SpaceConfig dataclass in embed_swap; have get_embed_config and the space_name branch both produce it; replace dict-key access with attribute access.

title: search() has 9 parameters including two boolean control flags
description: Current state: search() takes 9 parameters (conn, query, top_k, source_type, mode, keyword_prefilter, chunk_strategy, space_name, rerank, rerank_top_n) of which two are boolean mode-switches (keyword_prefilter line 208, rerank line 211) and several are optional knobs that only matter together (rerank_top_n only when rerank=True; space_name toggles a whole resolution branch). Boolean flags as parameters are a recognized smell: they signal the function does more than one thing and force callers to read positionally (search(conn, q, 10, None, 'hybrid', True, ...)). Target state: group the non-essential search knobs into a SearchOptions dataclass (keyword_prefilter, chunk_strategy, space_name, rerank, rerank_top_n, source_type) leaving conn/query/top_k as the core call. Pattern name: Introduce Parameter Object + Replace Boolean Flag. Motivation: distinct from the function-length finding — even after the body is decomposed, the wide flag-laden signature remains hard to call correctly and to extend. Estimated scope: signature change plus call-site updates (routes/search.py and tests). Risk: low-to-medium — public-ish API used by the search route, so callers must migrate; keep keyword-only defaults to ease transition.
suggested_fix: Introduce a SearchOptions dataclass (keyword-only fields with defaults) for the optional knobs; keep conn/query/top_k explicit. Optionally make the booleans keyword-only first as a cheap intermediate step.

title: Magic number 2 for folder-boost distance threshold lacks a named constant
description: Current state: line 166 computes threshold = best_distance * 2 if best_distance > 0 else 1e-6 — the multiplier 2 (and the 1e-6 floor) are unnamed magic numbers controlling which folders get boosted, while every other tuning knob in this module is a named module-level constant (RRF_K, FOLDER_BOOST_FACTOR, FOLDER_BOOST_TOP_N, SEARCH_OVERFETCH_MULTIPLIER, etc., lines 42-60) with an explanatory comment. The inline comment on line 164 ('boost folders within 2x of best') documents intent but the value is not extractable/tunable. Target state: FOLDER_BOOST_DISTANCE_THRESHOLD_MULTIPLIER = 2 and FOLDER_BOOST_MIN_DISTANCE = 1e-6 alongside the other folder-boost constants. Pattern name: Replace Magic Number with Symbolic Constant. Motivation: consistency with the module's own convention and tunability of a relevance-affecting heuristic. Estimated scope: 2-3 lines. Risk: none (pure rename of a literal to a constant).
suggested_fix: Add FOLDER_BOOST_DISTANCE_THRESHOLD_MULTIPLIER = 2 and FOLDER_BOOST_MIN_DISTANCE = 1e-6 near the other folder-boost constants and reference them on line 166.

title: Lazy in-function import of get_space despite embed_swap already imported at module top
description: Current state: line 258 does `from .embed_swap import get_space` inside the search() body, even though embed_swap is already imported at module level (line 16: `from .embed_swap import get_embed_config`). This is inconsistent: the rerank import at line 381 is a deliberate lazy import (heavy/optional dependency, wrapped in try/except for graceful degradation), but get_space is from a module already loaded eagerly, so the lazy form buys nothing and hides the dependency from the import block. Target state: add get_space to the line-16 module-level import. Pattern name: Hoist (consolidate) Import. Motivation: naming/locality consistency; a reader scanning module imports sees the full embed_swap coupling. Estimated scope: 1 line moved. Risk: none — embed_swap is already imported, so no new import cycle is introduced (confirm: search<-embed_swap edge already exists at line 16).
suggested_fix: Move `get_space` into the existing top-level `from .embed_swap import get_embed_config` import and delete the in-function import at line 258.

title: Redundant score_map rebuild from ranked before result construction
description: Current state: line 454 reassigns score_map = dict(ranked[:top_k]). Tracing the data flow: ranked was produced by sorted(score_map.items(), ...) at line 375 (and re-sorted at line 422 after rerank), so the existing score_map already contains every key present in ranked[:top_k] with the same up-to-date values. The reassignment therefore reconstructs a dict that is value-equivalent to the live score_map for all keys read in the loop at 457-471 (score_map[cid], cid in chunk_ids = ranked[:top_k]). It is correct, just superfluous. Target state: drop line 454 and rely on the existing score_map (or, if defensiveness is desired, leave it but document why). Pattern name: Remove Redundant Computation. Motivation: minor clarity — a re-derivation that looks meaningful but is a no-op invites future readers to hunt for a difference that does not exist. Estimated scope: 1 line. Risk: very low; verify no path mutates score_map between line 422 and 454 (none observed). Not dead code — frame as superfluous, not unreachable.
suggested_fix: Remove the line-454 reassignment; the loop can read the existing score_map populated through the boost/rerank phases.

title: Final chunk fetch hand-builds IN-clause placeholders instead of using _batched_select
description: Current state: the final detail fetch (lines 435-450) manually constructs placeholders = ','.join('?' * len(chunk_ids)) and assembles params/type_filter/strategy_filter by hand, whereas every other multi-id query in this module routes through db._batched_select with the {ph}/extra_params convention (lines 177, 197, 334, 389). This is an abstraction-level inconsistency. It is safe here only because top_k is capped at MAX_TOP_K=500 (line 60), so chunk_ids never exceeds SQLite's 999-variable limit — i.e. correctness rests on an invariant declared elsewhere in the file rather than on the batching abstraction the module otherwise uses. Target state: route the fetch through _batched_select with chunk_strategy/source_type as extra_params (note _batched_select preserves no ordering, but the code already re-orders via the chunk_ids loop at 457, so that is fine). Pattern name: Use Existing Abstraction / Remove Duplicated Mechanism. Motivation: module-coupling consistency and decoupling from the MAX_TOP_K invariant. Estimated scope: rewrite ~15 lines into one _batched_select call. Risk: low — must preserve the AND filters as trailing ? extra_params in the correct order. Not a bug at current cap; framed as consistency.
suggested_fix: Replace the manual placeholder/param assembly with a single _batched_select call carrying source_type/chunk_strategy via extra_params; keep the chunk_ids loop for ordering.

title: space_name (non-active space) search path is entirely untested
description: The `space_name` branch of `search()` resolves a specific embedding space via `get_space()`, builds `space_cfg` from the space's model/dim/provider, reads `matryoshka_base_dim`, sets a non-None `vec_table`, and computes `skip_folder_boost = not active or space['name'] != active['name']`. No test in tests/test_search.py, tests/test_reranker.py, tests/test_folder_summaries.py, or tests/test_embed_spaces.py ever calls `search(..., space_name=...)` (grep for `space_name=` in tests/ returns zero matches). This means the folder-boost-skip logic, the per-space provider/model selection, and the explicit `vec_table` plumbing into `_vec_search` are unverified. A regression that, e.g., applied folder boost to a non-active space (folder_summaries_vec is dimensioned for the active space) or queried the wrong vec table would not be caught.
suggested_fix: Add tests that create a second space (reuse `create_space`/`backfill_space`/`promote_space` helpers from test_embed_spaces.py), then call `search(conn, q, space_name='other')`. Assert (a) results come from the named space's vec table, (b) `_folder_boost` is NOT invoked when the named space != active (patch `knowledge_base.search._folder_boost` and assert_not_called), and (c) when space_name equals the active space name, folder boost IS still eligible.

title: Matryoshka query-embedding truncation branch is uncovered
description: When `space_base_dim` is truthy, `search()` embeds the query at `space_base_dim` then calls `truncate_embedding(query_embedding, space_cfg['dim'])` to shrink it to the space dim before vector search. Grep for `matryoshka` and `truncate_embedding` in tests/test_search.py and tests/test_embed_spaces.py returns no hits for this search code path — matryoshka spaces are tested at ingestion/backfill level (test_create_matryoshka_space, test_backfill_matryoshka_space) but never through `search()`. The truncation-at-query-time logic (and the subsequent None-guard before truncating) is unverified, so a mismatch between query-embedding dim and the stored vec-table dim would surface only at runtime.
suggested_fix: Create a matryoshka space (base_dim > dim), make it active, ingest a chunk, and call `search()`. Patch `knowledge_base.search.embed_single` to return a base_dim-length vector and assert the vector handed to `_vec_search` has been truncated to `space_cfg['dim']` (e.g. patch `_vec_search` and inspect the embedding length argument).

title: embed_single returning None (vec leg skipped) is untested
description: In both the matryoshka and non-matryoshka branches, `search()` guards with `if query_embedding is not None` before running `_vec_search` and before folder boost (line 369). All existing tests mock `embed_single`/`_fake_embed_single` to return a real vector, so the None path — where the embedding provider fails and the vec leg must be silently skipped while hybrid still returns FTS results — is never exercised. A regression that passed None into `_vec_search` (a `serialize_f32(None)` crash) or into `_folder_boost` would not be caught.
suggested_fix: Add a test patching `knowledge_base.search.embed_single` to return None, ingest a doc, and assert `search(conn, q, mode='hybrid')` returns FTS-only results without raising, and `search(conn, q, mode='vec')` returns [] gracefully.

title: Reranker graceful-degradation except clause is untested
description: The rerank block catches `(ImportError, RuntimeError, ValueError, OSError)` and falls back to RRF ordering with a logged warning. Existing reranker tests (test_reranker.py) only patch `knowledge_base.reranker.rerank` to succeed; grep shows no test forces it to raise. The fallback contract — results still returned in RRF order, match_type NOT 'reranked', warning logged — is unverified. If the except list ever narrowed or the fallback re-raised, no test would fail.
suggested_fix: Add a test patching `knowledge_base.reranker.rerank` with `side_effect=RuntimeError('inference failed')`, call `search(..., rerank=True)`, and assert results are non-empty, `match_type != 'reranked'`, and a warning is emitted (caplog). Parametrize over ImportError/RuntimeError/ValueError/OSError to lock the catch set.

title: FTS OperationalError (malformed MATCH query) skip path untested
description: `_fts_search` is wrapped in `try/except sqlite3.OperationalError` so that a malformed FTS5 MATCH expression (e.g. unbalanced quotes, bare boolean operators) skips the FTS leg instead of crashing. No test feeds a query that triggers an FTS5 syntax error; test_search_keyword_prefilter_stopword_only_query covers the empty-fts_query short-circuit (line 294) but not the OperationalError catch. A user-supplied query like `"unterminated` would hit this in production unverified.
suggested_fix: Add a test calling `search(conn, '"unbalanced quote', mode='hybrid')` (a query that raises sqlite3.OperationalError in FTS5) and assert it does not raise and falls back to the vec leg. Optionally also test mode='fts' returns [] for such a query.

title: _fetch_chunk_contents and _vec_search default-table path lack direct tests
description: `_fetch_chunk_contents` (lines 192-199) has no direct test (grep returns zero hits in tests/); it is only exercised transitively through the rerank-enabled search tests. `_vec_search` is tested directly only for the int8 element type (test_embed_spaces.py:517-519); the default `table_name=None` resolution via `get_vec_table_name(conn)` plus the float32 `ELEMENT_QUERY_EXPR` path is not directly unit-tested. These are batched-SQL boundary helpers (the 999-variable IN-clause limit lives in `_batched_select`), so a direct test guarding empty-input and large-input behavior is valuable.
suggested_fix: Add a focused unit test for `_fetch_chunk_contents(conn, [])` returning `{}` and for a >999-id list returning the full mapping (verifies batching). Add a `_vec_search` test on the default float32 active table asserting (chunk_id, distance) tuple shape and ordering by ascending distance.

title: Post-fetch chunk-id mismatch skip (source_type/strategy filter dropping candidates) untested
description: After ranking, `search()` re-fetches chunk rows with an optional `source_type`/`chunk_strategy` SQL filter, then iterates `chunk_ids` skipping any `cid not in chunk_map` (lines 457-459). This handles the case where a ranked candidate is filtered out at the final SELECT. test_search_source_type_filter exercises filtering for FTS-only on a non-matching type (returns 0), but the partial-mismatch case — where some ranked ids survive the final filter and others are silently dropped while preserving order/scores — is not asserted. The `score_map[cid]` lookup and ordered reconstruction (line 457 iterating original `chunk_ids`) is the load-bearing logic.
suggested_fix: Ingest a mix of pdf and markdown chunks that both rank, call `search(..., source_type='pdf')`, and assert (a) only pdf results return, (b) returned order matches the ranked order of surviving ids, and (c) each result's `.score` equals its score_map entry (not reindexed).

title: Public SearchResult dataclass has no docstring
description: SearchResult is exported as public API via __all__ (line 37) and is the return element type of the only public function search(), yet it has no class docstring describing its role as the search return type. The fields are partially self-documenting via names and type hints, and match_type carries an inline comment listing its four values ('fts', 'vec', 'hybrid', 'reranked'), but there is no narrative for the dataclass itself. SearchResult is also serialized to the MCP tool response by the routes/search.py layer, so its field semantics are visible to external callers.
suggested_fix: Add a one-line class docstring, e.g. '"""A single hybrid-search hit: a chunk plus its fusion/rerank score and provenance."""', and optionally document the field semantics (especially match_type and score) for consumers.

title: search() docstring omits conn parameter and lacks a Returns section
description: The Google-style docstring for the public search() function is missing two items relative to the signature. (1) The first parameter `conn: sqlite3.Connection` (line 203) is not documented in the Args section (lines 217-233) — all other nine parameters are. This is a common convention for elided DB-connection parameters, but it is a strict signature-vs-docstring gap. (2) There is no Returns section, even though the return type is the non-trivial `list[SearchResult]` (line 213); combined with SearchResult itself having no docstring, a consumer has no narrative on the returned value. The Raises section (lines 235-237) and all four ValidationError paths are accurate.
suggested_fix: Add an Args entry for `conn` (e.g. 'conn: Open SQLite connection to the knowledge-base DB.') and a Returns section (e.g. 'Returns: List of SearchResult hits ordered by descending score, length <= top_k.').

title: 6 dependencies with known CVEs (requests/urllib3/idna/pygments/pip)
description:
pip-audit (28 deps audited) — known CVEs:
| package | version | advisory | fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| idna | 3.11 | CVE-2026-45409 | 3.15 |
| pip | 26.0.1 | CVE-2026-3219 | 26.1 |
| pip | 26.0.1 | CVE-2026-6357 | 26.1 |
| pygments | 2.19.2 | CVE-2026-4539 | 2.20.0 |
| requests | 2.32.5 | CVE-2026-25645 | 2.33.0 |
| urllib3 | 2.6.3 | PYSEC-2026-142 | 2.7.0 |
| urllib3 | 2.6.3 | PYSEC-2026-141 | 2.7.0 |
requests/urllib3/idna are transitive (trafilatura) on web.py's HTTP/SSRF fetch path.
suggested_fix: Bump fixed versions: idna>=3.15, pygments>=2.20.0, requests>=2.33.0, urllib3>=2.7.0; upgrade pip>=26.1 in CI image. Then `uv lock` + verify.

title: No [tool.ruff]/[tool.mypy]/[tool.pyright] config — lint runs default ruleset only
description: pyproject.toml has no [tool.ruff], [tool.mypy], or [tool.pyright] section despite ruff/mypy/pyright all installed. Ruff therefore runs its DEFAULT minimal ruleset (E/F/W) — the Phase-1 '0 warnings' undersells real lint debt — and there is no enforced type checking (20 mypy errors exist today).
suggested_fix: Add [tool.ruff.lint] with a real select (E,F,W,B,SIM,UP,C4,PTH,RUF,S) and [tool.mypy] (or pyright config) + a CI gate. Triage the surfaced findings.

title: Direct deps use >= without upper bounds
description: httpx/numpy/pillow/sqlite-vec/trafilatura/... are pinned only with >= (no upper bound). Mitigated by uv.lock (present, 390KB) which pins exact versions for reproducible installs, so impact is low; flagged for SemVer-break awareness.
suggested_fix: Optionally add conservative upper bounds (e.g. <next-major) on load-bearing deps; or document that uv.lock is the reproducibility contract.

title: Loosely-typed defaultdict yields 11 mypy union-attr false positives
description: The entity_data defaultdict factory returns a heterogeneous dict ({'type':None,...,'mentions':[]}), so mypy widens the value type to list|None and flags .append/__iter__ on data['mentions'] (lines 607/629/648). This is NOT a runtime bug — mentions is always [] from the factory — but it is real type-debt that masks genuine None errors. Verified by reading the code.
suggested_fix: Define a TypedDict (e.g. _EntityData with mentions: list[_Mention]) and type the defaultdict factory, so mypy tracks per-key types.

title: valid_ids redefined (mypy no-redef) — shadows line 340
description: mypy [no-redef]: valid_ids is annotated/defined at line 340 (chunk_strategy pre-filter) and re-declared at 395 (rerank pre-filter). Benign today (disjoint scopes) but the shadow is a readability/typing smell; verify no logic overlap.
suggested_fix: Rename one (e.g. rerank_valid_ids) so the two filter passes don't share a name.

title: page_map redefined (mypy no-redef) — shadows line 299
description: mypy [no-redef]: page_map defined at 299 then re-declared at 302. Likely a conditional-branch reassignment mypy rejects; verify both branches intend the same variable.
suggested_fix: Unify the two assignments or rename; annotate once.
